Speaker 2: Review password policies, make sure default admin accounts are renamed, and log authentication events somewhere you can actually search. Speaker 1: Draft an incident contact tree now—who talks to investors, customers, regulators—so you are not scrambling mid-crisis. Speaker 2: And decide on vulnerability scanning cadence plus patch windows; expectations set early are easier to enforce later.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Backups and data protection

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify critical data stores: source code, CRM, finance, shared drives and product telemetry.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Verify at least one automated backup exists with retention that meets contractual promises.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item One startup lost three months of customer support tickets when their help desk vendor had a data center fire. They assumed “it’s SaaS, they handle backups” until the fine print said otherwise.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Test a sample restore quarterly and document who validated it, how long it took and what broke.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Outline manual fallback workflows—exporting CSVs, printing key documents, or switching to a secondary tool.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track compliance drivers (tax, privacy, contracts) that dictate how long data must stay recoverable.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Cloud-first vs. hybrid realities}

\index{Cloud-first vs. hybrid realities}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [outcome-focused] By the end of the workshop you should have tangible outputs, not just a lively chat. Speaker 2: That means a scored checklist, a 30/60/90 plan, refreshed runbooks and a folder of evidence screenshots and policies. Speaker 1: Book the follow-up review before everyone leaves the room—ideally before the next hire or investor update. Speaker 2: Treat it like any other deliverable: assign owners, due dates, and drop the tasks into your project tracker right away.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Cloud-first vs. hybrid realities

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Catalogue where workloads actually live: fully managed SaaS, cloud-native infrastructure or a closet server humming beside the coffee machine.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cloud-first startups lean on identity providers and vendor assurances—validate export options and incident SLAs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Hybrid environments demand network diagrams, VPN policies and clear ownership for patching on-prem gear.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Flag data residency constraints early; some investors or customers will demand proof of where data rests.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Budget reality check}

\index{Budget reality check}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [career-minded] These assessments are often championed by fractional CTOs, security-savvy ops managers or MSP onboarding leads. Speaker 2: It is a fantastic shadowing opportunity for junior analysts—they learn facilitation, stakeholder translation and control baselining. Speaker 1: The real skill is empathy: explaining why MFA matters without sounding like the “no” police. Speaker 2: Nail that and you build the muscle to grow into head of IT, risk lead or customer trust advocate roles as the company scales.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Budget reality check

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Triage controls by risk and runway: what must be solved with \$500/month versus what waits for a \$5K allocation.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight quick wins—enforcing MFA, password managers, baseline device hardening—before pricier SIEMs or MDR contracts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map spend to milestones (Series A, first enterprise customer) so finance understands why costs jump.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture deferred items with explicit triggers: “Upgrade logging when monthly recurring revenue hits \$250K.”
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Common day-zero pitfalls}

\index{Common day-zero pitfalls}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [encouraging] Keep the checklist alive; review it after every hire, vendor change or funding milestone. Speaker 2: When investors or auditors call, you already have evidence folders and owners lined up—it shifts the tone from defensive to confident. Speaker 1: More importantly, the team knows what “secure enough” looks like today and how it will mature tomorrow. Speaker 2: That shared playbook turns day-zero chaos into a calm, repeatable ritual that protects both momentum and trust.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Common day-zero pitfalls

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Relying on personal Gmail accounts for vendor contracts and Stripe access.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Skipping device encryption because “it’s just a prototype laptop.”
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assuming vendors manage backups, incident response or compliance obligations without written proof.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Forgetting to offboard contractors, leaving privileged accounts active for months.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treating security tasks as “best effort,” so they die in the backlog when sales gets hectic.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Vendor due diligence quick wins}

\index{Vendor due diligence quick wins}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Vendor due diligence quick wins

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Create a lightweight intake form covering data stored, access model, compliance attestations and breach history.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask for security documentation (SOC 2, pen test summary) before signing, not after the renewal.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Verify termination clauses: how fast can you retrieve or purge data when the contract ends?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Add vendors to your asset and identity maps so joiner/leaver workflows catch shared integrations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep a template questionnaire email ready to send the moment a new tool is proposed.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Security controls and monitoring}

\index{Security controls and monitoring}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security controls and monitoring

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review password policies, SSO coverage and whether default admin accounts have been renamed or disabled.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Confirm logging is enabled for auth events, financial transactions and production infrastructure.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Establish an incident contact tree with who calls legal, PR, investors and affected customers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set expectations for vulnerability scanning cadence and patch response windows.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture third-party vendor attestations (SOC 2, ISO 27001) and note renewals on the calendar.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 13: Workshop outputs}

\index{Workshop outputs}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Workshop outputs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item A scored checklist PDF or Notion page with red/amber/green status and named owners.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item A 30/60/90-day roadmap of remediation tasks aligned to risk and business milestones.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Updated runbooks: account lifecycle, device setup, backup testing and escalation paths.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Template email scripts for vendor security questionnaires and customer assurances.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Starter policy templates (access control, device, incident response) ready for quick customization.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Quick-reference cards for emergency procedures—lost device, suspected phishing, production outage.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item A shared folder with evidence—screenshots, policy links, vendor contracts—for future audits or funding rounds.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item A follow-up session booked to review progress before the next hire or investor meeting.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 14: Roles, traits and progression}

\index{Roles, traits and progression}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Roles, traits and progression

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Day-zero assessments are often led by fractional CTOs, security-minded operations managers or MSP onboarding leads.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Junior security analysts and IT generalists can shadow to learn stakeholder facilitation and control baselining.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Success hinges on curiosity, diplomacy and the ability to translate checkboxes into founder-friendly language.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item As the company scales, these practitioners grow into heads of IT, risk leaders or customer trust advocates with board visibility.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Encourage cross-functional allies—finance, HR, product—to own portions of the checklist so accountability is shared.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 15: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

A living day-zero checklist is your startup's safety net. It keeps identity, devices, backups and security posture honest from the outset, making future audits and incidents far less dramatic.

Treat the artefact as a shared playbook: iterate after every hire, vendor change or funding round so resilience matures alongside revenue.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Day-Zero Core Services Setup}
\index{Day-Zero Core Services Setup}
Day-zero sounds dramatic, but it's literally the first five business days. Exactly—incorporation, domains, devices and security all race to go live together. Miss a step and you're chasing...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Day-Zero Core Services Setup}

\index{Day-Zero Core Services Setup}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Day-zero sounds dramatic, but it's literally the first five business days. Speaker 2: Exactly—incorporation, domains, devices and security all race to go live together. Speaker 1: Miss a step and you're chasing paperwork while customers wait. Speaker 2: So we map the whole week before the first hire even signs their contract.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Day-Zero Core Services Setup

Launch the company without losing the first week

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: What "day zero" covers}

\index{What "day zero" covers}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What's actually included in this "day-zero" checklist? Speaker 2: Anything that makes the company real—legal filings, domains, baseline tooling and who owns each task. Speaker 1: So it's not just IT running off to configure email. Speaker 2: Right, it's a cross-functional sprint with evidence you can show an MSP, investor or auditor.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

What "day zero" covers

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Incorporation paperwork, domains, devices and baseline tooling
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Mapping who owns which setup tasks during the first 5 business days
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Getting identity, communication and knowledge systems online together
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capturing decisions so MSPs, investors and auditors see a coherent plan
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Incorporation \& registrations}

\index{Incorporation \& registrations}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: We start with the boring stuff: entity registration and bank accounts. Speaker 2: Boring until a contractor asks for payment and you realise payroll IDs aren't ready. Speaker 2: Or until a contractor sends an invoice and you discover "Awesome Startup LLC" was never actually registered. Speaker 1: Nothing kills the entrepreneur vibe faster than admitting you're technically a sole proprietorship. Speaker 1: Or a founder leaves and there was never a signed agreement. Speaker 2: That's why day-zero includes a data room folder for all those artefacts.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Incorporation \& registrations

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Lodge the legal entity, appoint directors and open a business bank account
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Secure tax IDs and payroll registration before the first contractor invoice lands
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document ownership structure and sign founder agreements to prevent later disputes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Create a data room folder for incorporation artefacts and board resolutions
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Domains, DNS and website plumbing}

\index{Domains, DNS and website plumbing}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Domains feel simple—just buy the .com and you're done, right? Speaker 2: Until someone forgets the .co or country code and a squatter grabs it. Speaker 1: Or when the CEO's ex-partner controls the domain and decides to get creative during the breakup. Speaker 2: That's why we register defensives—and use business email, not the founder's hotmail-from-college account. Speaker 1: Or the registrar is tied to a personal Gmail account you can't access during travel. Speaker 2: Shared ops email, templated DNS records and uptime monitoring keep launches from face-planting.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Domains, DNS and website plumbing

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Register primary domains plus defensives (.com, .co, relevant country codes)
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use registrar accounts tied to shared ops email, not a founder's personal inbox
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set up DNS hosting with templated records for MX, SPF, DKIM and verification tokens
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Configure uptime monitoring so a silent DNS change does not break email launches
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Productivity suite and identity backbone}

\index{Productivity suite and identity backbone}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Choosing Google Workspace versus Microsoft 365 still sparks debates. Speaker 2: The real question is which ecosystem your customers expect and what integrates with your stack. Speaker 1: Either way, MFA on admin roles and shared mailboxes can't wait a month. Speaker 2: And even if HR is a spreadsheet, sync it so joiners and leavers stay in lockstep.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Productivity suite and identity backbone

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Choose Google Workspace or Microsoft 365 based on customer expectations and toolchain fit
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example: a B2B SaaS startup picked Google Workspace because enterprise buyers expected Google SSO integration
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compare costs early—Google Workspace Business Standard at \$12/user/month vs Microsoft 365 Business Premium at \$22/user/month
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Establish a primary identity provider and enforce MFA on admin roles from day one
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Create shared mailboxes (hello@, finance@) and delegated calendar access for founders
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sync HR roster or interim spreadsheet to drive joiner/mover/leaver workflows
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Communication and knowledge hubs}

\index{Communication and knowledge hubs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Where do we keep the policies and meeting notes so they don't vanish in chat history? Speaker 2: Spin up a knowledge base on day one, even if it's a single-page Notion workspace. Speaker 1: And pre-build channels for incidents, board updates and customer escalations. Speaker 2: Templates save teams from reinventing emails at 2 a.m. when something breaks.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Communication and knowledge hubs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stand up chat (Slack/Teams) with channels for founders, delivery, customers and incidents
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Launch a lightweight knowledge base (Notion, Confluence, Google Sites) for policies and SOPs
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Decide where meeting notes, board decks and investor updates live to avoid scattered history
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-create announcement, incident and customer escalation templates for fast reuse
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Device procurement and setup}

\index{Device procurement and setup}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Hardware always turns up late unless you plan buffers. Speaker 2: Exactly—keep a few imaged laptops ready with asset tags and shipping labels. Speaker 1: And there's always one founder who insists on a \$4,000 gaming laptop "for better performance." Speaker 2: Which promptly gets coffee spilled on it during the first investor meeting. Speaker 1: And don't forget travel kits for sales or fundraising trips. Speaker 2: Record serials and warranties so replacements aren't a scavenger hunt.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Device procurement and setup

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Order a buffer of laptops with baseline images, asset tags and shipping templates
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Plan inventory—for a two-person team, order three laptops so a backup is ready for demos or travel hiccups
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget \$1,500–2,000 per laptop including warranty, MDM licensing and shipping
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-stage admin accounts in MDM or Autopilot/Zero Touch before boxes leave the supplier
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document loaner process plus travel kits (power adapters, privacy filters, LTE dongles)
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track serial numbers, warranty dates and assigned owners in the asset register
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Security guardrails on hour one}

\index{Security guardrails on hour one}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Security feels like overkill before the first customer signs. Speaker 2: Yet that's when attackers love to strike—defaults are still wide open. Speaker 1: So we turn on password managers, logging and break-glass accounts immediately. Speaker 2: And make sure founders know who to call—lawyers, insurers, incident responders—if something goes sideways.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security guardrails on hour one

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enable password manager, phishing reporting button and security awareness bite-size modules
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Turn on default logging, backup policies and conditional access before inviting new users
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Generate break-glass accounts with hardware keys stored off-site under dual control
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Recommend starter tooling: 1Password for teams (\$8/user/month), Microsoft Defender for Business or Google Workspace security center
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Add founders to incident bridge, legal counsel and cyber insurer contact lists
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Budgeting for day-zero services}

\index{Budgeting for day-zero services}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Remind me about Sarah's DNS incident—you keep telling teams that story. Speaker 2: She registered the domain with her personal email, deleted a wildcard record at 1 a.m. and the demo site vanished for six hours. Speaker 1: Investors called before breakfast and the sales team had to reschedule every meeting. Speaker 2: Now she keeps registrar access in a shared vault with change windows, even with ten employees.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Budgeting for day-zero services

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Reserve \$200–500/month for productivity suite licensing, domain registration and DNS hosting
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Allow 2–3 weeks for hardware procurement, imaging and inevitable shipping delays
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Line up \$1,000–3,000 for incorporation legal fees plus trademark searches
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-approve founder credit cards so vendor sign-ups are not stalled at payment screens
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Early compliance and data residency}

\index{Early compliance and data residency}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: How do we keep momentum once the checklist starts? Speaker 2: Daily stand-ups, a Kanban board and a link to evidence for every completed task. Speaker 1: Plus async walkthrough videos so the next hire isn't blocked waiting for a founder. Speaker 2: And note which lawyers, accountants or MSPs you escalate to if things stall.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Early compliance and data residency

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Confirm whether early contracts mandate specific data locations or certifications
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document which services store data in which regions (e.g. Slack US, email EU) to avoid surprises
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Draft a lightweight privacy policy before collecting any customer information
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Flag GDPR, CCPA or industry rules that influence vendor shortlists and architecture choices
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: When day-zero planning goes wrong}

\index{When day-zero planning goes wrong}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So the goal is confidence that core services survive founder vacations and audits. Speaker 2: Exactly—treat day-zero as a living runbook, not a one-off launch party. Speaker 1: When everything's documented, due diligence calls become show-and-tell. Speaker 2: And the team can focus on customers instead of chasing missing DNS logins.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When day-zero planning goes wrong

Real examples teach better than theoretical checklists.

Let's see how a simple DNS mistake nearly derailed a promising startup.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Sarah's DNS cautionary tale}

\index{Sarah's DNS cautionary tale}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Sarah's DNS cautionary tale

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sarah, the CEO, registered the domain under her personal email and "learned DNS" at 1 a.m.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item She deleted the wildcard record while experimenting, taking product demos offline for 6 hours
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales woke up to bounced customer emails and a frantic investor asking about the outage
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item The fix: shared registrar access, documented records and change windows even for tiny teams
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Slide 13: First-week runbook}

\index{First-week runbook}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

First-week runbook

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Create a simple Kanban board with day-zero tasks, owners and completion evidence links
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule daily 15-minute stand-ups to clear blockers and surface vendor delays
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Record walkthrough videos for critical systems so future hires ramp without live hand-holding
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Note dependencies on lawyers, accountants or MSPs and pre-book escalation contacts
\end{itemize}

\index{Key takeaway}

A disciplined day-zero setup makes incorporation, domains, devices and security feel intentional.

Treat the checklist as a living runbook that survives founder vacations, vendor turnover and the first due-diligence call.

\section{Working with Fractional CTOs and MSPs}
\index{Working with Fractional CTOs and MSPs}
Lean teams eventually hit a ceiling—product ambition outpaces leadership bandwidth. That's when fractional CTOs and MSP partners start appearing in board meeting minutes. The trick is to...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Working with Fractional CTOs and MSPs}

\index{Working with Fractional CTOs and MSPs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Lean teams eventually hit a ceiling—product ambition outpaces leadership bandwidth. Speaker 2: That's when fractional CTOs and MSP partners start appearing in board meeting minutes. Speaker 1: The trick is to use them to accelerate maturity, not to abdicate the hard decisions. Speaker 2: So today we unpack when to bring each partner in and the questions that keep expectations sane.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Working with Fractional CTOs and MSPs

Choosing the right leadership model for lean teams

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why fractional leadership exists}

\index{Why fractional leadership exists}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Founders usually wait too long to admit they need senior guidance. Speaker 2: Right—fractional leaders exist because hiring a permanent CTO takes months and equity you can't spare. Speaker 1: Virtual CIOs and MSPs handle different pain: governance, policy, 24/7 operations. Speaker 2: Most engagements land in the 6 to 18 month window—long enough to stabilise, short enough to keep urgency high. Speaker 1: When cash is tight, trade 0.5 to 1 percent equity for part-time leadership instead of a \$150k salary you can't cover yet. Speaker 2: Expect blended billing—retainers, day rates and per-incident fees—so model the spend before you commit.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why fractional leadership exists

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start-ups need senior judgment before they can afford full-time executives
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Fractional CTOs, virtual CIOs and MSPs fill different gaps across strategy, delivery and run operations
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Typical engagements run 6–18 months until hiring a permanent leader or internal capability matures
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Expect blended fees: day-rates for discovery, retainers for leadership time, per-incident or per-device MSP charges
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stage cues: pre-seed firms borrow architecture patterns, Series A scale to multi-region uptime, Series B demand compliance rigour and portfolio planning
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Equity trade-offs: 0.5–1\% advisor equity versus a \$150k cash salary keeps burn low when revenue is volatile
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Example: Series A SaaS Startup}

\index{Example: Series A SaaS Startup}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Let's sort out who to call based on the mess in front of you. Speaker 2: Product roadmap chaos? A fractional CTO sets architecture guardrails and mentors engineering leads. Speaker 1: Board grilling you on IT risk? A virtual CIO can own policy cadence while the MSP implements the controls. Speaker 2: And if pager duty is burning everyone out, the MSP has to anchor the help desk and incident response.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Example: Series A SaaS Startup

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Situation: 15-person team, scaling from 1K to 10K users in six months
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Challenge: CTO departed mid-migration, roadmap slipped, board mandated security audit
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Solution: 6-month fractional CTO to stabilise architecture and hiring paired with co-managed MSP for compliance automation
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Outcome: Permanent CTO hired, SOC 2 audit passed on schedule, roadmap velocity recovered without losing key customers
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: When to engage each partner type}

\index{When to engage each partner type}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Engagement shape matters just as much as who you hire. Speaker 2: An embedded fractional leader joins exec meetings weekly and steers hiring and architecture. Speaker 1: Some founders only need a six-week strategist to map the roadmap and hand off to their own team. Speaker 2: Co-managed MSPs keep product decisions in-house, but a full outsource risks skills atrophying if you don't stay engaged.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When to engage each partner type

SituationFractional CTOVirtual CIOManaged Service Provider

Product roadmap in flux[OK] Sets architecture guardrails, mentors engineering leads[Warning] Provides governance but less hands-on[Fail] Focuses on run operations

Board demanding IT controls[Warning] Can cover interim CIO duties[OK] Owns policy, risk and budgeting cadence[OK] Implements controls and monitoring

24/7 support gaps[Warning] Designs on-call model[Warning] Escalation owner[OK] Runs help desk, NOC and incident response

Major platform migration[OK] Leads technical decision-making[OK] Aligns roadmap with business priorities[OK] Supplies delivery squads and change management

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Typical Investment Levels}

\index{Typical Investment Levels}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Vet a fractional CTO like you would a permanent exec. Speaker 2: Ask which stages they've navigated—seed, Series B, messy turnarounds—and what outcomes they achieved. Speaker 1: Availability matters; if they juggle five clients, who shows up when your production outage hits? Speaker 2: Request artifacts—architecture memos, hiring scorecards—and learn whether they coach, architect or swoop in as a fixer.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Typical Investment Levels

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Fractional CTO: \$8K–15K/month for 1–2 days per week, often with ramp-down clauses
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Virtual CIO: \$5K–10K/month covering governance, budgeting and board prep
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Co-managed MSP: \$3K–8K/month plus per-incident fees for after-hours or specialised work
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget rule: Allocate 15–25\% of the engineering/IT budget to external leadership to avoid starving internal capability
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Equity swaps: Some leaders accept 0.25–0.5\% options in lieu of cash—model dilution before agreeing
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Partnership models and ownership}

\index{Partnership models and ownership}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: MSP due diligence can't stop at a glossy pitch deck. Speaker 2: Drill into incident response—who answers at 2 a.m. and what escalation path they follow. Speaker 1: Check their security posture and whether they'll integrate with your ticketing and SSO instead of adding silos. Speaker 2: And nail down the commercial model—after-hours rates, pass-through costs and the fine print on exit clauses.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Partnership models and ownership

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Embedded fractional leader: 1–2 days/week, attends exec meetings, drives architecture and hiring
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Project-based strategist: 4–6 week discovery, hands over roadmap to internal team or MSP
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Co-managed MSP: Provider runs service desk while founders retain product and security decisions; expect shared tooling within 30–60 days
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Full outsource: MSP controls infrastructure, releases and vendor management—risk of skill atrophy without oversight and longer re-entry timeline
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Transition milestones: Define 30-, 60- and 90-day checkpoints for documentation, tooling access and leadership cadence
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Evaluating fractional CTO candidates}

\index{Evaluating fractional CTO candidates}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Vendors love promising "unlimited" everything. Speaker 2: My favourite line is "we onboard in a week"—sure, if you don't mind copy-pasting scripts yourself. Speaker 1: Use humour to keep it human, but make them show the runbook, the ticket queue stats and who actually did the work. Speaker 2: When they boast "our AI monitors everything", I ask to see the alerts that drag them out of bed at 3 a.m. Speaker 1: And "seamless integration" translates to "tell me how many API calls your tooling will hammer our systems with". Speaker 2: If they can't laugh and still produce evidence, that's a red flag before you even sign.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Evaluating fractional CTO candidates

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item What stage experience do they have (seed, Series A, turnaround) and which outcomes did they deliver?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item How do they split time across clients and guarantee availability during incidents or board crunches?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask for artifacts: recent architecture memos, hiring scorecards, budget models
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Probe for collaboration style with in-house engineers and product leads—coach, architect, or fixer?
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Evaluating MSP partners}

\index{Evaluating MSP partners}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: References tell you how providers behave when things get messy. Speaker 2: Call past clients and ask how knowledge transfer went when the engagement ended. Speaker 1: Run a tabletop exercise before you sign—it reveals decision-making speed and tooling depth. Speaker 2: Don't forget subcontractors and insurance requirements; your customer contracts probably demand both.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Evaluating MSP partners

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Incident response expectations: who answers the 2 a.m. call and within which SLA window?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security posture: SOC 2, ISO 27001, background checks, tooling stack for monitoring and patching
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Integration depth: can they plug into your ticketing, SSO and asset inventory instead of running siloed tools?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Commercial transparency: rate cards for after-hours work, pass-through vendor costs, exit clause specifics
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: The vendor promise vs delivery gap}

\index{The vendor promise vs delivery gap}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Once the contract is signed, governance keeps everyone aligned. Speaker 2: Start with a RACI—who owns roadmap, change approvals, incident command and vendor spend. Speaker 1: Run quarterly reviews with shared dashboards so surprises surface early. Speaker 2: And agree on the exit plan now: documentation handover, credential rotation and how long they'll stay during transition.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

The vendor promise vs delivery gap

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales teams promise "unlimited support"—clarify concurrency limits and scope exclusions
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask how many startups of your size they actively serve; beware being the smallest fish
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Request real-world incident reviews: what went wrong, how communication flowed, lessons learned
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Humour helps: "Show me the runbook, not just the glossy brochure" sets tone without burning bridges
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item When they tout "AI monitoring everything", counter with "Great—show me the 3 a.m. alert stream and who triaged it"
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item If they promise "seamless integration", ask "How many API calls will your tooling make against our stack each hour?"
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Due diligence and reference checks}

\index{Due diligence and reference checks}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Bottom line—fractional leaders and MSPs buy you time, not absolution. Speaker 2: Use sharp evaluation questions and a bit of humour to expose gaps before they turn into incidents. Speaker 1: Then govern the partnership like any critical system with clear roles and exit plans. Speaker 2: For homework, draft five evaluation questions for your stage and swap them with a peer for feedback.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Due diligence and reference checks

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speak with at least two former clients about responsiveness and knowledge transfer at exit
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run a tabletop exercise during contracting to observe decision-making and tooling capabilities
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review subcontractor usage and ensure confidentiality clauses cover fractional leaders and MSP engineers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align insurance requirements (cyber, professional indemnity) with your customer contracts
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Protect intellectual property: stipulate source control access boundaries, invention assignment, and how strategic docs are stored
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Watch for red flags: vague SLAs, resistance to documentation handover, or reliance on a single engineer for critical services
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Onboarding and ongoing governance}

\index{Onboarding and ongoing governance}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Onboarding and ongoing governance

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Define RACI across roadmap ownership, change approvals, vendor spend and incident command
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule joint quarterly business reviews with metrics dashboards and backlog updates
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture intellectual property in shared repositories with clear licensing in contracts and shared editing norms
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Integrate external leaders into stand-ups, retros and architecture councils so internal teams retain voice and context
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Plan the exit: 30–60 day transition timeline, documentation handover and credential rotation
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track performance: operational SLAs met, roadmap throughput, hiring progress and internal capability uplift
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Red Flags and Exit Strategies}

\index{Red Flags and Exit Strategies}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Red Flags and Exit Strategies

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Warning signs: Repeatedly delayed incident responses, scope creep without change control, thin documentation despite reminders
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Exit planning: Keep 30-day notice baked into contracts, require handover checklists, insist on joint credential rotation
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Backup plans: Maintain internal runbooks for critical systems, cross-train staff, avoid single points of failure in access or knowledge
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 13: Industry, geography and scaling considerations}

\index{Industry, geography and scaling considerations}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Industry, geography and scaling considerations

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Healthcare \& FinTech: Ensure partners can evidence HIPAA, PCI-DSS or local privacy compliance and support security questionnaires
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item B2B SaaS: Demand customer assurance support—MSP-ready answers for SOC 2, ISO 27001, and shared trust portals
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Consumer apps: Focus on scaling infrastructure, CDN tuning and observability for viral usage spikes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Geographic spread: Align on time zones, language coverage and in-region data residency obligations
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Scaling decisions: Define metrics (lead time, defect escape rate, customer NPS) that trigger transition from fractional to full-time leadership
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 14: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

Fractional CTOs and MSPs can accelerate maturity when paired thoughtfully.

Use structured evaluation questions, humour to surface mismatched expectations and explicit governance to avoid abdication.

Assignment: Draft five evaluation questions tailored to your current stage and share them with a peer for critique.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Guest Speaker Ideas}
\index{Guest Speaker Ideas}
Guest Speaker Ideas — Narrative Welcoming guest voices into the start-up IT module keeps the material grounded in reality. The aim of this segment is to highlight three complementary...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Guest Speaker Ideas}

\index{Guest Speaker Ideas}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Guest Speaker Ideas — Narrative Welcoming guest voices into the start-up IT module keeps the material grounded in reality. The aim of this segment is to highlight three complementary perspectives that expose learners to leadership, operational delivery and investor expectations. By curating diverse experiences we show founders and early operators what "good" and "risky" look like beyond theory.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Guest Speaker Ideas

Voices that make the session tangible

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why bring guest voices?}

\index{Why bring guest voices?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Why bring guest voices? Open with the rationale: founders have endless frameworks but rarely hear candid accounts of what actually happened during a crunch. Emphasise that each speaker translates a different pressure point—technical firefighting, service delivery promises and the scrutiny of outside capital. Call out that the goal is not inspirational talks; it is to interrogate decisions and trade-offs.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why bring guest voices?

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Anchor theory in lived experience across fractional leadership, MSP delivery and venture due diligence
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide contrasting viewpoints on risk appetite, tooling investments and hiring choices
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Offer networking opportunities for learners seeking mentors or advisory support
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Humanise cautionary tales so teams remember the stakes when shortcuts seem tempting
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Fractional CTO perspective}

\index{Fractional CTO perspective}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Fractional CTO perspective Position the fractional CTO as the voice of experience when the wheels wobble. Have them walk through their first 90-day plan: stabilise the architecture, triage tech debt, prioritise hires and embed lightweight governance. Ask for stories contrasting a pre-seed engagement—where they're duct-taping shipping velocity—with a Series B client that needs compliance, forecasting and stakeholder management. Prompt them to discuss pitfalls like unclear decision rights, unpaid scope creep and what happens when teams assume an advisor is on-call 24/7. Close with a practical readiness checklist founders can complete before reaching out to fractional leaders.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Fractional CTO perspective

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share the "first 90 days" playbook: architecture triage, hiring priorities and governance rituals
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Contrast survival tactics for pre-seed scrappiness vs Series B controls
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight pitfalls: unclear decision rights, unpaid discovery work, treating advisory time as unlimited
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Leave learners with a one-page readiness checklist before engaging fractional leaders
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item They’ll help you understand why “it works on my machine” isn’t a deployment strategy
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Startup-focused MSP account manager}

\index{Startup-focused MSP account manager}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Startup-focused MSP account manager Introduce the MSP account manager as the operator who turns contracts into day-to-day coverage. Encourage them to deconstruct a typical co-managed support relationship: who fields which tickets, how they integrate tooling, what on-call escalation looks like in practice. Ask for anonymised SLA dashboards showing healthy and unhealthy trends so learners can interpret their own metrics. Cover pricing levers—per device, per user, compliance surcharges—and how those evolve with headcount or regulatory scope. Include guidance on running quarterly business reviews that focus on backlog burn-down and continuous improvement rather than endless upsells.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Startup-focused MSP account manager

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Illustrate how co-managed support actually runs—handoffs, tooling integration and after-hours cover
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Bring real SLA dashboards and explain what healthy vs risky metrics look like
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Discuss how pricing levers change with headcount, device mix and compliance demands
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Coach teams on running quarterly business reviews that stay actionable instead of salesy
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Learn the difference between “managed services” and “managed chaos”
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: VC diligence or portfolio operations lead}

\index{VC diligence or portfolio operations lead}

\paragraph{Narration.}

VC diligence or portfolio operations lead Frame the investor representative as a reality check on what external stakeholders scrutinise. Have them outline their diligence checklist: security controls, revenue instrumentation, resilience plans, staffing and cultural signals. Request anonymised red and green flag examples pulled from data-room reviews—missing access logs, surprise shadow IT, or great runbooks that sped up approval. Explore how IT maturity shifts valuation conversations, board confidence and follow-on funding decisions. Reinforce that the best preparation is building diligence-ready documentation and repeating tabletop drills long before a term sheet appears.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

VC diligence or portfolio operations lead

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Demystify what investors check: security posture, revenue instrumentation, resilience plans
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Explain how IT maturity influences valuation, board confidence and follow-on funding
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share anonymised red/green flags spotted during data-room reviews
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Encourage founders to build "diligence-ready" documentation habits from day one
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Discover why your investor’s technical due diligence isn’t just checking if you have Wi-Fi
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Logistics and prep tips}

\index{Logistics and prep tips}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Logistics and prep tips Spell out the operating rhythm so organisers are not scrambling. Recommend sourcing speakers 6–8 weeks in advance, confirming NDAs, slide-sharing permissions and accessibility needs. Pair each guest with a learner moderator responsible for research, intros and audience questions; schedule a 30-minute prep call to align on flow. Provide context briefs that summarise audience maturity, session goals and no-go topics. Finally, plan to capture the session for reuse—obtain consent, organise recording and editing support, and publish assets to the cohort hub with clear access controls.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Logistics and prep tips

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Source speakers 6–8 weeks ahead; confirm NDAs and slide usage rights early
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pair each guest with a learner moderator and prep call agenda
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide context briefs: audience maturity, time limits, desired takeaways
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture sessions for reuse—secure consent, handle editing, and publish to the cohort hub
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Call to action}

\index{Call to action}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Call to action Close the segment by nudging learners to practice outreach. Ask them to draft an email to their dream guest—highlighting the topic fit, proposed format, audience size and how they will make the speaker's time worthwhile. Invite a few volunteers to read their drafts and workshop improvements live. Reinforce that thoughtful preparation and a clear value exchange dramatically increase the hit rate when approaching busy leaders.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Call to action

Ask learners to draft an outreach email to their top-choice speaker and outline the value exchange offered.

We workshop the best pitches live to build confidence in making the ask.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Preparing for Investor Due Diligence}
\index{Preparing for Investor Due Diligence}
Sarah's seed deck promised investors she could scale without burning the place down—or turning the office into a literal or metaphorical dumpster fire; now Series A questions are landing...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Preparing for Investor Due Diligence}

\index{Preparing for Investor Due Diligence}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [confident] Sarah's seed deck promised investors she could scale without burning the place down—or turning the office into a literal or metaphorical dumpster fire; now Series A questions are landing in her inbox daily. Speaker 2: The slides we’re about to walk through are the playbook for proving that promise isn’t just marketing glitter. Speaker 1: Think of due diligence prep like running production change management—clear owners, change logs and rollback plans. Speaker 2: And just like change management, the calm comes from having evidence ready before anything breaks in front of the board.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Preparing for Investor Due Diligence

Give investors confidence that controls match the story

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why diligence heats up post-Seed}

\index{Why diligence heats up post-Seed}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [analytical] Series A partners now assume you've got discipline around finance, security and customer retention. Speaker 2: When they ask, "Show me your churn cohorts and your incident history," they're testing whether growth has guardrails. Speaker 1: That pivot from pipeline to pen tests is their way of confirming discipline, so the fastest way to erode confidence is to stall or improvise—every "let me get back to you" adds friction to the deal. Speaker 2: That’s why we start aligning evidence months before the outreach email ever hits an investor’s inbox.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why diligence heats up post-Seed

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Series A/B investors expect proof that security, finance and compliance scale with revenue.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Unanswered questions slow down term sheets and spook co-investors.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sarah's seed deck promised "enterprise-ready"—now she must show receipts, not artisanal buzzword salad.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Preparing early buys founders time when the data room inevitably expands.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Core workstreams to coordinate}

\index{Core workstreams to coordinate}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [structured] We break diligence into three streams so nothing falls between departments. Speaker 2: Finance owns cash, contracts and SOC 2 timelines; security tracks access controls and incidents; product captures roadmap and customer promises. Speaker 1: A program manager or chief of staff keeps everyone aligned, running the same cadence as sprint reviews—finance demos burn forecasts, security closes the "12 admin accounts" story, product shows roadmap deltas. Speaker 2: Weekly stand-ups and a single tracker give investors confidence that the team can execute across silos.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Core workstreams to coordinate

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Financial \& operational – cash runway, burn, vendor commitments, SOC 2 roadmap.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security \& infrastructure – access controls, incident history, backup testing evidence.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Product \& customers – roadmap dependencies, SLAs, churn and expansion metrics.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assign an owner per stream (CFO, CTO, RevOps) with a single program manager stitching updates together.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Building a living data room}

\index{Building a living data room}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [practical] Treat the data room like a product release—versioned, documented and curated, with a changelog that shows new evidence landing every week. Speaker 2: Every file needs a cover note explaining what it is, why it matters and the last review date, plus a contact person if investors want a deeper dive. Speaker 1: Sarah colour-codes action items with due dates so investors see momentum instead of a pile of gaps, and she tags blockers that need board attention. Speaker 2: Sensitive exports stay in watermark-enabled folders with access logs; no more "here's a spreadsheet" emails floating around, and expirations auto-trigger reminders to revoke access.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Building a living data room

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Centralise policies, architecture diagrams, vendor contracts and board minutes with version control.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Include short context notes so outsiders understand why a document matters.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track open actions with due dates—investors value visibility more than perfection.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep sensitive exports (e.g. customer lists) in controlled folders with watermarking and access logs.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Security questionnaire watch-outs}

\index{Security questionnaire watch-outs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [alert] Security questionnaires look intimidating, but most questions repeat: MFA, pen tests, backups, privacy. Speaker 2: We front-load our red flags—shared admin accounts, missing asset inventories—so investors see honesty, not surprise. Speaker 1: Each gap gets a mitigation plan with owner, budget and timeline; for example, "12 privileged accounts without MFA, YubiKey rollout funded at \$15k and complete by June 30." No vague "we're working on it" responses. Speaker 2: A plain-language FAQ helps translate acronyms into outcomes for investors and board members who aren’t security natives.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security questionnaire watch-outs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify common asks: MFA coverage, penetration test cadence, disaster recovery drills, privacy compliance.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Expect specifics like "What percentage of privileged accounts enforce MFA?"—"82\% today, moving to 100\% by Q2 via YubiKeys" beats evasive answers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Flag red signals early—shared admin accounts, missing asset inventory, stale incident response plans.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Draft honest mitigation plans instead of hand-waving; investors reward realism.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain a FAQ that translates technical control names into plain language for partners and board members.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Map policies to governance expectations}

\index{Map policies to governance expectations}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [governed] Investors want to know who actually signs off on risk. Speaker 2: We map every policy to a board sponsor or advisor—security charter to the risk chair, finance controls to the audit lead. Speaker 1: Decision logs show when exceptions were approved and by whom; it proves governance is active, not theoretical, and highlights when legal or product weighed in. Speaker 2: A shared calendar connecting audits, board reviews and certification renewals keeps everyone in rhythm, with reminders like "Q2 risk committee + SOC 2 dry run" baked in. Speaker 1: When the partner asks "Who closes the loop?" we point to the governance RACI and invite them to our next tabletop recap. Speaker 2: That transparency signals maturity—it feels like they're joining an existing cadence instead of inventing one mid diligence.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Map policies to governance expectations

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie each policy to the board committee or advisor who sponsors it (e.g. audit, risk, security).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Summarise decision rights: who approves exceptions, how often reviews occur, what evidence is logged.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight how legal, finance and engineering collaborate on compliance checkpoints.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share a governance calendar: Q1 risk committee + SOC 2 readiness review, Q2 audit committee + PCI scan, Q3 full board + cyber tabletop, Q4 certification renewals.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Evidence and metrics investors trust}

\index{Evidence and metrics investors trust}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [data-driven] Dashboards alone won't close a round, but they anchor the story in facts. Speaker 2: Quarterly posture reports, uptime achievements and MTTR trends show resilience in motion—"99.6\% SLA hit, 12-minute MTTR" reads better than buzzwords. Speaker 1: Pair the charts with narratives—"here’s the remediation we shipped after that pen test"—so investors hear accountability and progress. Speaker 2: We also surface how the risk register feeds Jira or Notion; governance without execution is just wallpaper, and burndown charts prove tasks actually close.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Evidence and metrics investors trust

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share quarterly security posture reports, uptime SLAs achieved (99.5\%+ is Series A table stakes, 99.9\% a stretch goal), mean-time-to-recover trends.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Bundle SOC 2 gap assessments, vulnerability remediation stats (e.g. 95\% of critical vulns closed <14 days) and third-party attestations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pair qualitative narratives with dashboards so numbers land with context.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Show how risk registers flow into product and operations backlogs for execution.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Rehearse the diligence conversation}

\index{Rehearse the diligence conversation}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [coaching] Before the real investors arrive, we run a mock session with our advisors playing the tough crowd. Speaker 2: Each exec practices a two-sentence answer with a pointer to deeper artefacts—no wandering monologues. Speaker 1: We record the rehearsal, capture follow-up tasks and assign owners in the tracker within the hour. Speaker 2: That loop builds muscle memory so the actual diligence call feels like a rerun, not improv night.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Rehearse the diligence conversation

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run a mock Q\&A with advisors posing as investors; record it for coaching.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Equip every exec with a "two-sentence answer + escalation" script for their domain—"Our incident response playbook assigns roles within 15 minutes, then hands to the CISO-led war room; want to see the drill notes?".
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Prepare backup slides for deeper dives—architecture, vendor matrix, privacy controls.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Log follow-ups immediately so nothing slips between meetings.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Roles, traits and progression}

\index{Roles, traits and progression}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [career-minded] Startups lean on program managers or chiefs of staff to keep diligence humming. Speaker 2: They partner with security or compliance leads who can translate questionnaires into sprint-sized work. Speaker 1: Finance and RevOps double-check the numbers and customer obligations so nothing surprises the board. Speaker 2: People who thrive here love diplomacy and structured storytelling—the same skills that lead to VP Ops or trust leadership roles later.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Roles, traits and progression

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Program manager / Chief of staff – orchestrates data room updates, keeps stakeholders aligned. Typical comp: \$140k–\$190k + equity at Series A/B.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security or compliance lead – translates questionnaires into actionable backlog items. Expect \$160k–\$210k, often paired with bonus tied to audit milestones.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Finance \& RevOps partners – validate metrics and customer contract obligations; senior managers sit \$130k–\$170k with upside at close.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Thrives on diplomacy, attention to detail and appetite for structured storytelling.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Career paths lead to VP Operations, Head of Trust \& Safety or venture portfolio advisor roles.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map progression milestones (owning first diligence cycle, leading certification renewals, joining board meetings) so the team sees a runway.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Legal and regulatory readiness}

\index{Legal and regulatory readiness}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [wrap-up] The big lesson—start six months early and treat diligence like an ongoing product, not a last-minute fire drill. Speaker 2: When policies, metrics and board oversight line up, investors feel like they're joining a machine that already runs. Speaker 1: Red flags are inevitable, but owning them with a remediation plan shows maturity, not weakness. Speaker 2: And that transparency is exactly what keeps term sheets moving instead of gathering dust in legal review.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Legal and regulatory readiness

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Catalogue applicable regulations early: GDPR/UK GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, HIPAA or SOC 2 depending on vertical.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document lawful bases for processing, data retention standards and DPA coverage for every critical vendor.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Show international scaling awareness—data residency in the EU, onshore support SLAs for APAC, breach notification variations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Partner legal and security leads on a quarterly compliance checkpoint so surprises surface before term sheet negotiations.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Timeline and critical path}

\index{Timeline and critical path}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Timeline and critical path

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Typical diligence cycles span 6–10 weeks from data room access to close; plan backward from your cash runway.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Week 1–2: data room review and follow-up questions; Week 3–5: deep dives with functional leaders; Week 6–8: confirmatory audits and customer calls.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight dependencies—SOC 2 Type II report delivery, customer reference availability, legal opinion drafting.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain a RAID log so risks, assumptions, issues and decisions stay visible to executives and investors.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Case study: NimbusPay Series A}

\index{Case study: NimbusPay Series A}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Case study: NimbusPay Series A

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Day 0: pre-seeded data room with 120 curated artifacts, ownership tracker in Notion.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Day 14: investors flagged MFA gaps; remediation plan committed to 100\% hardware keys in 45 days with \$15k budget.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Day 35: cross-border payroll expansion triggered GDPR transfer impact assessment and Canadian PIPEDA review.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Day 52: diligence closed after mock board review confirmed policy-to-governance alignment and incident drill readiness.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 13: Red flags hall of fame (and fixes)}

\index{Red flags hall of fame (and fixes)}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Red flags hall of fame (and fixes)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item "Security lead" is a contractor 5 hours/week → solution: interim virtual CISO backed by engineering manager accountable for controls.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item No incident response drill since founding → schedule tabletop within 30 days, document after-action and add to board pack.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer data stored in shared S3 bucket with ex-employee access → run access audit, enable object lock, revoke stale keys same week.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Legal can't articulate data residency commitments → map contractual obligations, document sub-processors, update privacy notice.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\index{Resources and templates}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

Resources and templates

\end{itemize}

\item Recommended tools: Tugboat Logic or Drata for control evidence, Notion/Confluence for playbooks, Vanta-style dashboards for KPIs.

\subsection*{Slide 15: Key takeaways}

\index{Key takeaways}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaways

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start gathering evidence six months before you fundraise; aim for annual-physical calm, not emergency-room chaos.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat the data room as a living product with owners, release notes and guardrails.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Red flags are inevitable—own them, show remediation progress and connect it to board oversight.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Investor confidence grows when policies, metrics and narratives reinforce one another.
\end{itemize}

\section{Legal and Compliance Reality Check}
\index{Legal and Compliance Reality Check}
We keep saying "we'll sort compliance after launch" but the enterprise pilot is already asking for controls. Exactly why this session exists—let's map what "good enough" looks like so we...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Legal and Compliance Reality Check}

\index{Legal and Compliance Reality Check}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: We keep saying "we'll sort compliance after launch" but the enterprise pilot is already asking for controls. Speaker 2: Exactly why this session exists—let's map what "good enough" looks like so we stop sprinting blind.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Legal and Compliance Reality Check

Keeping founders honest about the rules even when the roadmap is sprinting

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why this matters before Series A}

\index{Why this matters before Series A}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: SOC 2 feels mythical—people say it takes years and a room full of consultants. Speaker 2: Type I can land in a quarter if we assign an owner, reuse templates and rehearse evidence pulls monthly. Speaker 1: And Type II? Speaker 2: Plan on nine months because auditors watch controls run; automation for access reviews and logging keeps it sane.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why this matters before Series A

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Contracts, audits and enterprise pilots die quickly if you cannot evidence basic compliance hygiene.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Regulators and investors expect intent documented early, even if your stack is still duct tape and shared logins.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Getting ahead of obligations keeps trust intact with customers sharing sensitive data and engineers shipping fast.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Milestones on a lean compliance roadmap}

\index{Milestones on a lean compliance roadmap}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: We're pulling in GPL, Apache and MIT libraries without thinking—what's the real risk? Speaker 2: Licences travel with your code; at minimum we owe attribution, and GPL triggers source disclosures if our product distributes binaries. Speaker 1: So we need a log of what we're using? Speaker 2: Yes, a lightweight SBOM and approval step keep surprises out of vendor questionnaires and customer audits.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Milestones on a lean compliance roadmap

MilestoneWhat "good" looks likeTypical timeline

Founding (0-3 months)Policies drafted, access reviews tracked in a spreadsheet, vendor DPIAs noted.2-4 working days of concentrated effort

SOC 2 Type IControls designed and evidenced for a point-in-time audit; readiness assessment signed off.3-4 months with external coach

SOC 2 Type IIControl operation proven over 3-6 months, automation in place for logs and reviews.9-12 months from kickoff

ISO 27001 certRisk register, Statement of Applicability, internal audits and management review documented.12-15 months with staged scope

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Making audits survivable for small teams}

\index{Making audits survivable for small teams}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Our product team wants to ship a new analytics feature tomorrow—privacy review feels like a blocker. Speaker 2: Run the 15-minute DPIA template, strip optional personal fields and document consent flows now; it's faster than rewriting code post-incident. Speaker 1: Do we loop legal in on every tweak? Speaker 2: Bring them in for cross-border data moves or sensitive categories, but empower squads with reusable checklists for the routine cases.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Making audits survivable for small teams

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with a single owner (often COO, security lead or fractional CISO) plus one project manager or chief of staff.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use lightweight tooling: ticket queue for control tasks, password manager exports, MDM screenshots and change logs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Rehearse evidence pulls monthly so nothing lives only in someone's inbox or head.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate what you can early—cloud security posture tools, HRIS-to-IdP sync, log retention policies—before the audit gap list grows.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Open source licence obligations in production}

\index{Open source licence obligations in production}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Who actually owns this when we're only twenty people? Speaker 2: A fractional CISO or ops lead can captain it, with a privacy counsel on retainer and an engineer automating the evidence pulls. Speaker 1: What's the growth path for someone who loves this work? Speaker 2: Start as compliance coordinator, step into trust and safety leadership, and grow toward head of risk once the company scales.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Open source licence obligations in production

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM) and record licence types for each dependency.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item For copyleft (GPL) components, document how you provide source and any network interaction obligations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Apache and MIT libraries still require attribution—ship a NOTICE file in your repo and product help centre.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track obligations during vendor assessments: security questionnaires often probe licence posture.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assign an owner to approve new libraries and keep dependency updates tied to security patch cycles.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Privacy by design when you're shipping fast}

\index{Privacy by design when you're shipping fast}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Privacy by design when you're shipping fast

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map personal data flows for each new feature, noting storage, processors and retention choices.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run quick DPIA templates before launch—15 minutes to log risks, mitigations and approvals beats retrofitting controls later.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Default to data minimisation: drop optional fields, anonymise analytics and isolate test data from production.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Build consent and deletion journeys as reusable components so every squad does not reinvent them under pressure.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep legal counsel or an external advisor in the loop for cross-border data moves and sector-specific rules.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Roles, traits and progression}

\index{Roles, traits and progression}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Roles, traits and progression

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Key roles: fractional CISOs, security program managers, privacy counsels and compliance-minded ops leads.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Entry pathways: support engineers who wrangle audits, paralegals moving into tech, security analysts stepping into governance.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Traits: meticulous note-taking, calm stakeholder management, ability to translate legal clauses for engineers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Progression: from compliance coordinator to trust \& safety lead, then head of security governance or VP of risk as the company scales.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Quick-start checklist for founders}

\index{Quick-start checklist for founders}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Quick-start checklist for founders

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Appoint a single compliance captain with a documented backup.
