Action checklist

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Audit remote onboarding artefacts and fill gaps before the next cohort starts
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sign agreements with regional logistics and e-waste partners now
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate offboarding triggers for payroll, SaaS and physical assets
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review quiet hours, stipends and retreat budgets every quarter
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Remote Talent Logistics at Scale}
\index{Remote Talent Logistics at Scale}
Once you pass the first hundred remote hires, the "we'll figure it out" era ends. Exactly. Logistics becomes a product—you ship experiences, not just laptops. Our goal in this session is...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Remote Talent Logistics at Scale}

\index{Remote Talent Logistics at Scale}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Once you pass the first hundred remote hires, the "we'll figure it out" era ends. Speaker 2: Exactly. Logistics becomes a product—you ship experiences, not just laptops. Speaker 1: Our goal in this session is to replace heroics with systems that scale without burning people out. Speaker 2: Think of it as upgrading from a craft table to a production line while keeping the same care for each new teammate.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Remote Talent Logistics at Scale

Keeping distributed teams productive as headcount multiplies

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: From hustle to repeatable systems}

\index{From hustle to repeatable systems}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Remember when three people approved every laptop and the spreadsheet lived on someone's desktop? Speaker 2: That breaks the moment you scale to six countries and run parallel hiring sprints. Speaker 1: We need clear personas, automation triggers and regional partners ready before the next wave hits. Speaker 2: The promise is bold: day-two productivity without begging for favours across time zones.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

From hustle to repeatable systems

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item The "one-ops-person" era breaks once you cross \textasciitilde{}80 contributors in 6+ countries
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Logistics must cover hiring surges, contractor rotations and leadership travel simultaneously
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Standard kits, automation and regional partners turn chaos into predictable cycle times
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Goal: every hire is productive in 48 hours without manual heroics
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Hardware standards that actually scale}

\index{Hardware standards that actually scale}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Personas are our anchor—engineers, customer advocates, executives each need a standard kit. Speaker 2: Publishing SKUs, accessories and MDM policies keeps procurement and finance aligned. Speaker 1: It also means we can hold 5–10\% buffer inventory per region without guesswork. Speaker 2: Quarterly vendor reviews let us refresh specs while keeping the automation scripts intact.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Hardware standards that actually scale

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Publish persona kits (engineer, CX, exec) with approved SKUs, accessories and MDM baselines
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain 5–10\% buffer inventory per region using bonded warehouses or MSP lockers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ship pre-imaged devices with tamper seals plus welcome packs; record serials in asset CMDB
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule quarterly vendor reviews to refresh specs without derailing procurement
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Lifecycle logistics and recovery}

\index{Lifecycle logistics and recovery}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Shipping is only half the battle; we need visibility from purchase order to first login. Speaker 2: A shared dashboard shows customs holds, delivery confirmations and first-day check-ins. Speaker 1: When something breaks, regional depots with prepaid return labels make swaps painless. Speaker 2: And don't forget the back end—automated warranty claims and certified e-waste partners close the loop.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Lifecycle logistics and recovery

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Central dashboard tracks shipment status, customs holds and first-day confirmation
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Swaps flow through regional depots with prepaid return labels and wipe certificates
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate warranty claims via distributor portals; push updates to finance for capex tracking
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Contract e-waste partners on each continent for compliant disposals and donations
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Automated access provisioning}

\index{Automated access provisioning}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Hardware without access is just an expensive paperweight. Speaker 2: HRIS triggers push identities through SCIM into Okta or Entra, bundling the right apps per persona. Speaker 1: For technical teams we rely on infrastructure-as-code to grant scoped secrets and repos. Speaker 2: Even service accounts get expiry dates—no more zombie credentials haunting audits.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Automated access provisioning

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item HRIS/ATS triggers create identities via SCIM into Okta/AAD plus baseline app bundles
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Infrastructure-as-code applies least-privilege roles and scoped secrets for technical teams
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Service accounts auto-expire with contracts; mobile device enrollment enforces zero trust posture
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Joiner/mover/leaver playbooks live in runbooks with RTO and RPO targets per access class
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Access reviews on autopilot}

\index{Access reviews on autopilot}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Provisioning is great, but who checks access six months later? Speaker 2: Quarterly attestations inside the IAM tool keep managers accountable with usage data baked in. Speaker 1: High-risk systems drop to 30-day reviews with dual approvals so nothing slips. Speaker 2: Every remediation produces an audit-ready trail—tickets, timestamps and revoked roles.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Access reviews on autopilot

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Quarterly attestations route to managers inside the IAM tool with pre-filled usage signals
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Flag dormant or over-privileged accounts for auto-suspension after grace windows
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map critical systems (finance, source, prod) to 30-day reviews and dual approvals
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Export reports for auditors with evidence of remediation and ticket links
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Payroll, benefits and compliance integrations}

\index{Payroll, benefits and compliance integrations}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Logistics isn't only devices—payroll and benefits shape trust just as much. Speaker 2: Integrating Deel, Remote or Papaya with the HRIS means contracts, taxes and payslips land on time. Speaker 1: Benefits aggregators help localise wellness stipends and statutory coverage without manual spreadsheets. Speaker 2: Syncing holidays and time-off feeds prevents payroll from deducting leave twice for the same festival.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Payroll, benefits and compliance integrations

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Connect Deel/Remote/Papaya to HRIS for contract drafting, tax setup and payslip distribution
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Layer benefits aggregators (Ben, Forma, Humaans) to localise stipends and statutory coverage
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sync time-off calendars and statutory holidays into scheduling tools to avoid payroll errors
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain data residency maps; limit PII replication across finance, HR and IT systems
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Culture that scales with geography}

\index{Culture that scales with geography}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Technology only works if culture keeps pace with geography. Speaker 2: Regional ambassadors run welcome rituals, wellness budgets and office hours in native time zones. Speaker 1: Leadership rotations and quarterly meetups signal visibility without forcing relocation. Speaker 2: Written playbooks on etiquette and holiday swaps stop HQ norms from steamrolling local practices.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Culture that scales with geography

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget quarterly regional meetups; rotate leadership visits to reinforce visibility
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Local champions own onboarding rituals, wellness stipends and office hours in native time zones
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Publish cultural playbooks covering meeting etiquette, feedback norms and holiday swaps
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Blend async storytelling (Loom, Notion) with live celebrations to prevent HQ gravity
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Metrics to steer the machine}

\index{Metrics to steer the machine}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What do we watch to know the machine is working? Speaker 2: Start with time-to-productive—device ready and core access within 48 hours. Speaker 1: Layer access drift metrics and payroll accuracy so finance, security and people ops share one scorecard. Speaker 2: Pair it with employee pulse surveys; logistics CSAT and attrition by region show where the experience cracks.

Metrics to steer the machine

\item Time-to-productive: hardware ready + core tool access within 48 hours of start date

\item Access drift: \% of accounts needing remediation during quarterly review cycles

\item Global payroll accuracy: error rates per country plus support ticket volume
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Employee experience pulse: logistic CSAT, inclusivity scores and attrition by region
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\section{Scaling Support Processes}
\index{Scaling Support Processes}
Remember when founders personally reset Wi-Fi routers? That was charming at 10 people, but now it blocks product roadmaps. Exactly. We're here to show why scaling support is a strategic...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Scaling Support Processes}

\index{Scaling Support Processes}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Remember when founders personally reset Wi-Fi routers? That was charming at 10 people, but now it blocks product roadmaps. Speaker 2: Exactly. We're here to show why scaling support is a strategic investment, not just cleaning up after everyone else. Speaker 1: We'll move from chaos to a predictable service desk that earns trust from executives, auditors and customers alike. Speaker 2: And we’ll do it without copying enterprise bureaucracy—this is about the minimum viable maturity that still scales.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Scaling Support Processes

Evolving from founder-led fixes to a scalable service desk

> "We've all been there—the company's 'IT department' is whoever sits closest to the router."

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: When ad-hoc support stops working}

\index{When ad-hoc support stops working}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: First clue you’ve outgrown ad hoc support? Slack DMs turn into a roulette wheel of "Did anyone pick this up?" Speaker 2: My other favorite—new hires shadow for a week because there’s no knowledge base, just tribal lore from the first sysadmin. Speaker 1: Finance notices too. Without ticket data there’s no way to justify headcount or tool spend. Speaker 2: And compliance folks get nervous when you can’t produce incident logs during a customer audit. That’s the burning platform for change. Speaker 1: Meanwhile remote teammates wait overnight for laptop fixes because coverage lives in one time zone.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When ad-hoc support stops working

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Slack pings overwhelm the one IT generalist and create invisible queues
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Context lives in heads; onboarding a new hire takes days of oral history
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Regulators and auditors ask for incident logs you can't produce
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer-facing teams feel the pain first—every outage escalates straight to engineering
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Remote teammates are stranded without time-zone coverage or device access policies
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Picture this: It's 3 PM Friday, the CEO can't access email, and three teammates troubleshoot in parallel because there's no ticketing system
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Design the first help desk intentionally}

\index{Design the first help desk intentionally}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: The temptation is to just buy a tool, but the first step is designing intake and triage. Speaker 2: Right—choose one doorway for tickets. Portal plus email alias, both feeding the same queue with required fields. Speaker 1: Then define what "good" looks like: simple SLAs and an escalation ladder. Even a Trello board can work if the process is crisp. Speaker 2: Daily standups make invisible work visible, and a weekly retro keeps the backlog honest. Tooling only amplifies that discipline.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Design the first help desk intentionally

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Launch a single intake (portal + email) with auto-triage by request type
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Publish lightweight SLAs: critical 2h, high 4h, normal 1 business day
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Create macros for the top 20 requests; everything else routes to a backlog
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Implement daily standups and a weekly ops review to surface blockers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document remote/hybrid playbooks: shipping devices, break/fix couriers, regional on-call rotations
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Knowledge base as force multiplier}

\index{Knowledge base as force multiplier}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Knowledge bases fail when they become graveyards. We want a living system tied to ticket closure. Speaker 2: Exactly—agents draft the article while the fix is fresh, and SMEs review it during their Friday hour of power. Speaker 1: Short videos and annotated screenshots beat long prose for startup teams moving fast. Speaker 2: And don’t forget analytics. Track search terms with zero results, then prioritize new content from that list. Speaker 1: Once that foundation is in place, we can talk tooling choices that reinforce it instead of creating another content graveyard.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Knowledge base as force multiplier

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pair every resolved ticket with an article draft before closure
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use "seed, grow, prune" cycles: SMEs review monthly, archive stale pages quarterly
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Embed short Loom walkthroughs—startups learn visually faster than reading PDFs
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track self-service deflection rate and article helpfulness to justify more authors
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Take password resets: one article with screenshots can deflect 80\% of those 10-a-day Slack pings
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Tooling choices: ServiceNow vs Jira Service Management}

\index{Tooling choices: ServiceNow vs Jira Service Management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Let’s tackle the tooling debate. ServiceNow or Jira Service Management—what’s the difference in practice? Speaker 2: ServiceNow shines when you need rigid workflows, integrated CMDB and audit-grade change control. But it demands budget and a specialist admin. Speaker 1: Jira Service Management snaps into existing Atlassian workflows and ships with great automation and developer visibility. Speaker 2: The trade-off? You may need marketplace apps for CMDB depth and more governance features. So we map regulatory needs, current stack, and admin skills before deciding.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Tooling choices: ServiceNow vs Jira Service Management

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item ServiceNow: enterprise-grade workflows, deep CMDB, strong audit trails; higher cost and implementation lift
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Jira Service Management: native with Atlassian stack, faster to deploy, rich automation rules; needs add-ons for mature CMDB
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Decision guardrails framework:
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Current team size and 18-month growth trajectory
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Existing ecosystem integrations and API/library maturity
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compliance or audit obligations (SOC 2, SOX, HIPAA) and segregation of duties
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Available admin expertise and realistic implementation runway
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Bridge gaps with integrations (e.g., Slack virtual agent, asset DB sync) before committing to a rip-and-replace
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Layer automation and integrations early}

\index{Layer automation and integrations early}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Tool choice settled, the next lever is automation. Otherwise the team becomes human routers. Speaker 2: Start simple—Slack or Teams forms that capture device, urgency and screenshots, then auto-tag the ticket. Speaker 1: Sync asset data from MDM nightly so agents trust the CMDB when troubleshooting. Speaker 2: And hook the workflow engine to HR events so joiner, mover and leaver tasks fire automatically. That’s hours back every week. Speaker 1: Wrap those flows with security checks—privileged access reviews and phishing simulations—so operations and security grow together.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Layer automation and integrations early

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Connect Slack or Teams to create tickets with structured forms and context capture
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enforce change approvals and incident postmortems via workflow gates
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sync asset inventory (Intune, Kandji, Jamf) to the ITSM CMDB nightly
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate onboarding/offboarding tasks with workflow engine + IDP events
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Include security guardrails: privileged access reviews, phishing simulations, and incident response playbooks
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Staffing for maturity}

\index{Staffing for maturity}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Processes mean nothing without the right people at the right time. Speaker 2: Stage one, under 50 staff, you likely have a single operations lead wearing every hat. Give them clear escalation paths into engineering. Speaker 1: Stage two adds dedicated L1 agents and someone curating knowledge. Rotating product squads for L2 keeps context fresh. Speaker 2: By stage three you need specialists—security, infrastructure, SaaS owners—and a service owner measuring CSAT and backlog health.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Staffing for maturity

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stage 1 ($\le$50 staff): one ops lead covering L1 + process design; rotate engineers for escalation duty
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stage 2 (50–150): dedicated L1 agents, part-time knowledge manager, on-call matrix with product squads
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stage 3 (150+): L2 specialists for infra/security/apps, service owner, tooling admin, CSAT analyst
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Invest in career ladders and certification paths to retain institutional knowledge
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item "Stage 1 IT person: part technician, part therapist, part mind reader. At least give them proper escalation paths!"
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Process milestones to hit}

\index{Process milestones to hit}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Founders love roadmaps, so translate process maturity into month-by-month wins. Speaker 2: Month one, document services and publish runbooks for the top incidents. Month two, launch the knowledge base and start change reviews. Speaker 1: Month three brings problem management huddles and automated joiner/mover/leaver workflows. Speaker 2: After that, quarterly service reviews with finance and product keep the desk aligned to business priorities and budgets. Speaker 1: Call out failure modes at each stage so teams spot drift early—ownerless runbooks, ignored dashboards, automation without monitoring.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Process milestones to hit

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Month 1: catalog services, define categories, publish runbooks for top incidents — failure mode: no owners assigned, so runbooks rot
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Month 2: launch knowledge base, implement change calendar, start ticket QA program — failure mode: KB traffic untracked, execs lose faith
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Month 3: introduce problem management reviews, automate joiner/mover/leaver flows — failure mode: automation breaks without monitoring
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Month 4+: quarterly service reviews with finance/product, refresh tooling roadmap — failure mode: reviews drift into status theatre, not decisions
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Metrics that prove maturity}

\index{Metrics that prove maturity}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Metrics prove the desk is worth the investment. Without them, it’s just more overhead. Speaker 2: Track response and resolution SLAs, but also self-service deflection—can at least a third of tickets resolve without human hands? Speaker 1: CSAT surveys and article helpfulness scores show quality, while cost-per-ticket and engineering hours returned show business impact. Speaker 2: Package those results into a monthly narrative so executives keep funding headcount and tooling improvements. Speaker 1: With the numbers telling the story, our wrap-up can focus on reinforcing the habits that keep the desk evolving. Speaker 2: And bring a simple ROI one-pager to budget reviews so leaders see the cost avoidance alongside the spend.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Metrics that prove maturity

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ticket intake mix: aim for $\ge$30\% self-service deflection within six months
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Responsiveness: 90th percentile response under SLA, backlog <1.5× weekly throughput
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Quality: CSAT $\ge$4.5/5 and knowledge article helpfulness $\ge$80\%
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Business impact: downtime minutes prevented, engineering time saved, audit findings closed
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security: mean time to revoke access after offboarding and phishing report-to-response time
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Budget justification toolkit}

\index{Budget justification toolkit}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So the playbook is simple: single intake, living knowledge base, right-sized tooling, and people who can grow with the process. Speaker 2: Nail those and you turn IT from a fire brigade into a service your startup brags about in due diligence calls. Speaker 1: Plus the metrics make future investments easier to pitch. Nothing beats saying, "We saved 200 engineering hours last quarter." Speaker 2: Now let’s push the pilot live and iterate weekly. Momentum is your best stakeholder management tool. Speaker 1: Keep an eye out for the classic pitfalls—tooling without process, dusty runbooks, and remote teams left out of the loop—and course-correct fast.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Budget justification toolkit

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Build a one-page ROI summary: tickets deflected, hours saved, compliance risks reduced
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Include a lightweight CapEx/OpEx model (licenses, headcount, contractors) with 3-year outlook
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie asks to business OKRs and upcoming audits; include "do nothing" risk column
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Borrow customer quotes or incident anecdotes to humanize the spend
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Common implementation pitfalls (and how to avoid them)}

\index{Common implementation pitfalls (and how to avoid them)}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Common implementation pitfalls (and how to avoid them)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tooling-first rollouts without process design → run a pilot with clear exit criteria
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ignoring change management → over-communicate via town halls, office hours, and exec sponsors
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Skipping data hygiene → schedule quarterly CMDB and knowledge audits with metrics owners
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Forgetting remote teammates → create follow-the-sun coverage and hardware depots in key regions
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Action checklist}

\index{Action checklist}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Action checklist

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item [ ] Pick pilot team, map top 10 request types and pain points
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item [ ] Stand up intake, SLAs and runbook templates before announcing new process
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item [ ] Select tooling with a weighted scorecard and run a two-week sandbox bake-off
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item [ ] Review metrics weekly; adapt staffing and knowledge strategy every quarter
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}


\section{Security Baselines on a Shoestring}
\index{Security Baselines on a Shoestring}
Imagine your seed-stage startup spending more on coffee than on security tooling. Yet the board still expects you to survive a phishing email or a stolen laptop without dialing 911 for IT....

\subsection*{Slide 1: Security Baselines on a Shoestring}

\index{Security Baselines on a Shoestring}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [inviting] Imagine your seed-stage startup spending more on coffee than on security tooling. Speaker 2: Yet the board still expects you to survive a phishing email or a stolen laptop without dialing 911 for IT. Speaker 1: We will decode the jargon, translate compliance checklists, and show which controls actually buy you sleep. Speaker 2: Think of us as your pragmatic advisor and technical translator, tag-teaming to stretch every security dollar.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security Baselines on a Shoestring

Keep founders secure without burning the runway

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security debt accrues interest; early baselines keep future compliance projects from exploding in cost.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compliance basics like GDPR Article 32 or SOC 2 CC6 expect controls even at ten-person scale; ignoring them stalls enterprise momentum.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Security 101: Threats that sink startups}

\index{Security 101: Threats that sink startups}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [pragmatic] First, let’s level-set the threat landscape—phishing, ransomware and accidental leaks do not wait for Series B funding. Speaker 2: Customers know it too, which is why GDPR clauses and SOC 2 questionnaires now hit before the second sales call. Speaker 1: A written baseline becomes the playbook you hand contractors, fractional CISOs and auditors so everyone enforces the same guardrails. Speaker 2: With that context set, we can prioritize the handful of controls that stop the bleeding fastest.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security 101: Threats that sink startups

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Phishing is the con-artist email that steals passwords and invoices; ransomware locks files until you pay in crypto; insider mistakes leak customer data without malice.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Attackers do not check your runway before blasting campaigns—see the 2023 Mailchimp breach that let intruders pivot into countless small SaaS companies.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item One lost laptop without encryption can trigger GDPR breach notices or torpedo a SOC 2 deal worth six figures of ARR.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Baselines do not guarantee invincibility, but they stack the odds so that common incidents are expensive inconveniences instead of existential crises.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security debt accrues interest; early baselines keep future compliance projects from exploding in cost.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compliance basics like GDPR Article 32 or SOC 2 CC6 expect controls even at ten-person scale; ignoring them stalls enterprise momentum.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Security jargon decoder for new IT pros}

\index{Security jargon decoder for new IT pros}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [focused] Start with four anchors: phishing-resistant MFA, a shared password vault, automatic patching and resilient 3-2-1 backups. Speaker 2: If you cannot prove who logged in, whether the laptop was healthy, or that data is recoverable, every other control is theatre. Speaker 1: Hardware keys for admins and \$20-per-user password managers are cheaper than the revenue lost during a forced credential reset week. Speaker 2: Nail these basics and you are ready to treat identity as the new perimeter, which is exactly where we go next.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Security jargon decoder for new IT pros

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item MFA (multi-factor authentication): extra proofs like a YubiKey or authenticator app layered on top of passwords so stolen credentials alone fail.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item SOC (security operations centre): humans plus tooling watching telemetry to spot and respond to suspicious activity—think night-shift security guards with dashboards.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item SIEM (security information and event management): the log brain collecting alerts from identity, endpoint and cloud systems for the SOC to interpret.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item MDM (mobile device management): software that enforces encryption, patching and remote wipe on laptops and phones—even from a beach Wi-Fi connection.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Zero trust: the philosophy that every access request must prove it is legitimate, no matter the network location or job title.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Why baselines still matter when cash is tight}

\index{Why baselines still matter when cash is tight}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [analytical] Identity is the control plane, so treat Workspace or Entra ID as the perimeter you can actually defend. Speaker 2: Our technical translation: block legacy auth, require healthy devices, and script joiner-mover-leaver flows so access changes within minutes. Speaker 1: Quarterly access reviews become storytelling moments—"here’s who lost admin rights and how we mitigated the risk." Speaker 2: With accounts locked down, it’s time to harden the laptops and phones people carry into coffee shops.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why baselines still matter when cash is tight

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Investors, enterprise customers and cyber insurers now bake security questionnaires into contracts; failing them can delay six-figure deals by months.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Real incidents are brutal: Code42 reported \$4M in recovery costs after a ransomware hit, and password reuse enabled the LastPass breach that rippled through startups for weeks.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Regulations like GDPR and US state privacy laws mandate timely breach reporting and fines; strong baselines reduce both likelihood and penalties.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sleep matters: leaders who know patching, backups and access reviews are humming can focus on product, not worst-case tabletop fantasies at 2 a.m.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Documented guardrails turn ad-hoc heroics into repeatable habits that contractors, MSPs and auditors can understand instantly.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Essential controls triage (with real stakes)}

\index{Essential controls triage (with real stakes)}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [grounded] Devices are still where breaches begin, especially when the team is scattered across kitchen tables and coworking hubs. Speaker 2: Lightweight MDM like JumpCloud or Kandji enforces encryption, patch automation and remote wipe for less than a nice lunch. Speaker 1: Pre-built recovery kits mean a stolen laptop triggers a one-hour replacement play, not a two-week GDPR panic. Speaker 2: Once endpoints behave, we can tackle the SaaS sprawl and "don’t tell mom" apps that hide outside IT.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Essential controls triage (with real stakes)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with MFA everywhere: hardware keys for admin roles and phishing-resistant app prompts for staff; a \$70 YubiKey beats weeks of resetting compromised SaaS portals.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Mandate a team password manager so shared credentials and API tokens live in audited vaults; cite the LastPass cascade as the cautionary tale for storing secrets in docs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate patching by enabling auto-update channels and alerting on drift—missing a browser patch enabled the 0-day that hit Uber’s contractor in 2022.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Follow the 3-2-1 backup pattern with offline snapshots so ransomware can’t wipe both production and synced cloud drives in one shot.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Expect to spend \$15–\$25 per person monthly for MFA, password management and backup tooling combined—less than catered coffee yet business-saving.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Identity and access on a lean budget}

\index{Identity and access on a lean budget}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [strategic] SaaS bloat sneaks up faster than payroll, so shine a light on every subscription and browser plug-in. Speaker 2: Finance exports, SSO logs and discovery add-ons expose the "free" tools bypassing MFA, logging and data retention commitments. Speaker 1: We pair that visibility with DNS filtering—the remote-friendly firewall that blocks malware domains before anyone clicks. Speaker 2: With the app layer tidy, we can decide whether to build detection in-house or rent a virtual SOC bench.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Identity and access on a lean budget

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use Google Workspace or Entra ID as the identity backbone, enforcing zero-trust defaults like blocking legacy IMAP and requiring device compliance for sensitive apps.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Configure conditional access like a bouncer who actually checks IDs, evaluating location, device health and role before opening the velvet rope.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate joiner/mover/leaver steps with HRIS webhooks or low-code scripts so account creation and revocation happen within minutes, not when someone remembers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Demonstrate cost-benefit: a \$6/month automation beats the \$30k bill from a disgruntled ex-contractor who retained production access.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run quarterly least-privilege reviews with founders and functional leads so business context guides access trims instead of blunt, confusing removals.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Device security without a full IT team}

\index{Device security without a full IT team}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [reassuring] You do not need a 24/7 internal SOC; you need trustworthy humans on retainer who know your environment. Speaker 2: Huntress, Arctic Wolf or Defendify drop straight into Slack with curated alerts and human analysts translating the noise. Speaker 1: Negotiate the playbook now—who calls whom at 2 a.m., how fast they escalate, and what evidence they collect. Speaker 2: Keep an internal owner accountable so the MSSP augments your team instead of becoming an expensive scapegoat.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Device security without a full IT team

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Adopt lightweight MDM such as Kandji, JumpCloud or Intune Business Premium for <\$10 per user; they handle encryption enforcement, firewall, screen-lock timers and lost-device wipes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share the coffee-shop cautionary tale: a stolen laptop once forced a two-week scramble because no remote wipe existed—MDM turned the sequel into a two-hour non-event.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-register hardware keys and recovery contacts in the MDM so replacements ship ready to go, avoiding frantic setup calls during an incident.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Segment update rings to push critical patches within 24 hours, with dashboards that flag stragglers before auditors or malware do.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document DIY checklists for founders covering "power wash and redeploy" steps so basic remediation doesn’t require heroics from the sole IT contractor.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: SaaS and network hygiene (with a wink)}

\index{SaaS and network hygiene (with a wink)}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [technical] Even on a budget we can centralise the signals that matter by treating telemetry as our flight recorder. Speaker 2: Wazuh, Elastic Agent or Panther Community keep costs low while Tines-style automation enriches alerts with owner, criticality and runbook links. Speaker 1: Prioritise identity, endpoint and cloud audit trails—the logs that tell us who did what, where and when. Speaker 2: Once those breadcrumbs are flowing, the culture work kicks in to make every teammate part of the detection surface.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

SaaS and network hygiene (with a wink)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Centralise SaaS discovery using finance exports, browser extensions and SSO logs to expose the "don’t tell mom" shadow IT apps before compliance teams do.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Force SSO or at least MFA on critical services; disable password-plus-email logins where possible so phishing kits meet dead ends.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Deploy DNS filtering via NextDNS or Cloudflare Teams as the remote-friendly firewall that blocks malware domains without shipping firewalls to apartments.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Catalogue vendor data locations, default retention and breach playbooks so customer questionnaires and GDPR Article 30 records are answered without panic.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sprinkle humour in onboarding: "Security hygiene—like dental hygiene but with fewer cavities and more credentials." It sticks better than policy PDFs.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Outsourced detection and monitoring options}

\index{Outsourced detection and monitoring options}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [playful] Remember the classic "have you tried turning it off and on again?" We weaponise that humour for patch hygiene. Speaker 2: Scheduled reboot windows, phishing drill shout-outs and coffee vouchers for first reporters make security feel winnable, not punitive. Speaker 1: Publishing MFA and patch scoreboards sparks friendly competition, proving culture change without shame. Speaker 2: That energy sets the stage for calm incident response rehearsals instead of panicked, once-a-year checkbox exercises.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Outsourced detection and monitoring options

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Virtual SOC subscriptions such as Arctic Wolf, Huntress or Defendify start near \$1,000/month for sub-50 seat companies—cheaper than even a part-time analyst.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Demand service-level clarity: 24/7 alerting, one-hour escalation on critical issues, and monthly playbook alignment sessions to keep context fresh.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share a scenario: "Arctic Wolf flags suspicious PowerShell on the marketing laptop—do we investigate or snooze?" Walk the leadership team through the response path now.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat MSSPs as staff augmentation by assigning an internal owner who reviews reports, tunes detections and ensures lessons become updated baselines.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Bundle basic compliance outputs—GDPR incident logs, SOC 2 evidence folders—so outsourced monitoring directly supports audits rather than adding parallel work.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Lightweight telemetry stack (aka digital breadcrumbs)}

\index{Lightweight telemetry stack (aka digital breadcrumbs)}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [calm] Preparation is the cheapest resilience—two-page runbooks, crisis comms templates and a speed-dial list beat Slack archaeology at 3 a.m. Speaker 2: Free tabletop guides from CISA or your insurer give you structure without consultancy rates or slide decks thicker than the product roadmap. Speaker 1: Capture lessons learned immediately so scripts, automations and contact trees evolve alongside the business. Speaker 2: Those notes flow straight into the roadmap we’ll walk through next, keeping momentum without overwhelming lean teams.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Lightweight telemetry stack (aka digital breadcrumbs)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Telemetry is your flight recorder: aggregated logs that reconstruct who did what, when, and from where when something smells off.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Prioritise identity, endpoint and cloud audit trails first; skip the noisy firewall syslog until you have people to interpret it.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Choose approachable tooling—open-source Wazuh or Elastic Agent, or affordable SaaS like Panther community or Tines workflows—that integrates alerts and automation without six-figure spend.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set retention tiers such as 30 days of hot searchable data and 180 days of cold storage to satisfy regulator or customer inquiries without blowing S3 budgets.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enrich alerts automatically with asset owner, data sensitivity and relevant runbook links so responders can move from "what happened?" to "what’s next?" in minutes.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Everyday hygiene culture (keep it human)}

\index{Everyday hygiene culture (keep it human)}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [motivating] Sequencing keeps the workload sane—MFA, vaulting and inventory in the first sprint, then SOC contracts and table-tops as confidence grows. Speaker 2: By day 60 the outsourced analysts know your escalation path; by day 90 you are iterating telemetry instead of firefighting. Speaker 1: Wrap every sprint with metrics: MFA coverage, patch SLAs, incidents closed internally versus escalated. Speaker 2: Those benchmarks become the board slide that proves security spend is disciplined, compliant and revenue-enabling.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Everyday hygiene culture (keep it human)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Embed the "have you tried turning it off and on again?" meme into operations hours to normalize quick patch restarts instead of procrastination.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Publish a monthly hygiene scoreboard showing MFA coverage, patch compliance and phishing-sim click rates; celebrate the teams hitting targets with shout-outs, not shaming.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Rotate mini-drills so every department experiences a simulated password reset, lost device or SaaS lockout—muscle memory beats policy binders during stress.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Encourage "see something, say something" with Slack emojis or forms for suspicious emails; reward the first reporter with coffee vouchers to reinforce good instincts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Remind the crew: "Security hygiene—like dental hygiene but with fewer cavities and more credentials." Repetition cements culture.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Incident readiness without big spend}

\index{Incident readiness without big spend}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Incident readiness without big spend

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Draft two-page runbooks for ransomware, account takeover and lost devices, mapping decision trees, evidence collection and external counsel contacts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-stage crisis comms templates, insurance hotline numbers and legal counsel retainer agreements so you are not googling at 3 a.m. under duress.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule quarterly tabletop exercises using free CISA or vendor guides; assign clear narrator, responder and observer roles to maintain engagement.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture lessons learned in a shared playbook repository, updating baselines, automation scripts and vendor contacts immediately.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align digital forensics steps with legal hold obligations early; even startups may face discovery requests from enterprise customers or regulators.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\index{Real-world failure stories (and recoveries)}

Real-world failure stories (and recoveries)

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Case 1: A Series A fintech lost six months of revenue when ransomware encrypted production and backups stored in the same cloud zone—3-2-1 backups would have cut downtime to hours.

\item Case 2: A biotech lost a seven-figure pharma partnership after failing a basic SOC 2 questionnaire because only 40\% of staff used MFA; remediation plus proof regained trust the following quarter.

\item Share these stories to underline that controls are not academic—they protect cash flow, contracts and credibility.
\end{itemize}

30/60/90-day implementation roadmap

\subsection*{Slide 4: Identity \& access: Okta as the spine}

\index{Identity \& access: Okta as the spine}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [analytic] Here’s the \$2.1K snapshot—identity, collaboration, delivery, compliance and RevOps. Speaker 2: Note the assumptions: 45 Okta seats, 30 Slack Business+ seats, 18 Zoom hosts. Speaker 1: Investors want to see the maths, so we show the per-seat logic and credits applied to Vanta. Speaker 2: Plus a path to stay inside burn modelling even when Snowflake usage spikes.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Identity \& access: Okta as the spine

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Deploy Okta Workforce Identity or Auth0 Workforce for workforce SSO.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enforce SAML for Slack, Zoom, Atlassian and HRIS in one dashboard.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate joiner/mover/leaver with HR triggers (Rippling, Deel, HiBob).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture MFA posture reports for diligence rooms.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget buffer: +\$120/month for adaptive policies or Advanced Server Access if engineers need SSH brokering.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Remember: if someone leaves and can still access Slack three days later, you're not building a security culture—you're building a revenge thriller.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

Speaker 2: And the audit trail is gold; diligence teams can literally download access reports and see policy history. Speaker 1: Offboarding is my devil's-advocate test—if someone can still hit Slack three days later, congratulations, you've produced a revenge thriller, not a security posture. Communication \& meeting layer

\subsection*{Slide 10: Serverless vs container run costs (monthly)}

\index{Serverless vs container run costs (monthly)}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Tools handled—now stress-test architecture costs before finance does the math. Speaker 2: Cost, latency, staffing, risk, credits; if those five pillars wobble, the rest of your board narrative collapses fast in seconds.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Serverless vs container run costs (monthly)

ItemServerless baselineContainer baseline

ComputeAWS Lambda \& API Gateway (\textasciitilde{}60M requests) = \$220EKS w/ 3 × m5.large nodes = \$340

DataDynamoDB on-demand \& S3 = \$90RDS Postgres + EFS backups = \$160

OperationsObservability (CloudWatch + Lumigo) = \$70Observability (Datadog APM + Logs) = \$180

Staffing0.5 platform engineer (shared)1 FTE platform/SRE for cluster upkeep

Total\$380 + shared headcount\$680 + dedicated headcount

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example scenario: fintech API handling 80M monthly transactions with market-hour spikes—serverless absorbs bursts, while containers force you to pay for 23 hours of idle capacity.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Serverless stays cheaper until workloads exceed 100M requests/month or need long-running compute.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Containers make sense when you require custom networking, GPU jobs or predictable workloads.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Decision triggers for architecture shifts}

\index{Decision triggers for architecture shifts}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [forward-looking] Data is the new argument—finance, RevOps and product need the same truth source. Speaker 2: Fivetran Lite pulls in SaaS data, Snowflake stores it cheaply and dbt applies the business rules. Speaker 1: We pause warehouse compute overnight to keep the bill under \$80 and alert if credits spike. Speaker 2: Reverse ETL comes later, but we already note the vendor shortlist so the roadmap feels intentional.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Decision triggers for architecture shifts

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Switch to containers when cold starts hurt SLAs or per-request cost exceeds \$0.60 per 1K invocations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget for Terraform/Terragrunt and cluster hardening audits before migrating.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pilot with managed Fargate/ECS to avoid Kubernetes overhead unless you already have deep ops talent.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document the total cost of ownership—tools, observability, people—in the board pack.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Real case: GrowthCo hit \$0.62 per 1K Lambda invocations and 150ms cold starts; six-week Fargate migration cut costs 40\% but required hiring a \$160K/year platform engineer.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Vendor evaluation checklist}

\index{Vendor evaluation checklist}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [comparative] Let’s crunch the infrastructure choice—serverless lands around \$380 in platform fees. Speaker 2: Think of the fintech API handling 80M monthly transactions; Lambda flexes with market spikes while containers would sit idle 23 hours a day. Speaker 1: Devil's advocate question—are we chasing Kubernetes because investors said "enterprise", or because the workloads truly need it? Speaker 2: Until you see 100M requests, brutal cold starts or custom networking, stay serverless and pour the savings into customer-facing capability.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Vendor evaluation checklist

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compare downgrade paths, integration APIs and roadmap transparency in a two-page matrix for leadership.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask for customer references facing your exact regulator before signing multi-year deals.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track exit clauses and data export guarantees in a shared deal room to avoid compliance surprises.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 13: Framing spend for skeptical investors}

\index{Framing spend for skeptical investors}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [decisive] When cost per 1K invocations creeps past \$0.60 or cold starts breach 150ms, the math flips. Speaker 2: GrowthCo hit both thresholds; six weeks on Fargate cut request spend 40\% but they also hired a \$160K platform engineer. Speaker 1: Devil's advocate—do we have Terraform discipline and security reviews ready, or are we just buying shinier compute? Speaker 2: Document that full TCO in the board pack so no one forgets the people cost hidden in the migration.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Framing spend for skeptical investors

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie each tool to a risk removed: SSO stops account sprawl, Vanta prevents \$200K SOC 2 consulting.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Show savings vs headcount: one security hire + in-house ETL exceeds \$25K/month fully loaded.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Present a time-to-value chart: Okta deployed in 3 weeks, Vanta audit ready in 90 days, Atlassian reporting in 1 sprint.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight scalability: all contracts scale to 100 seats without renegotiation.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Build an exit plan slide noting downgrade paths if growth slows.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item When investors ask "why not build it ourselves?", show them the fantasy salary for a security engineer who also runs RevOps, incident response and data pipelines. Spoiler: that person doesn't exist.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\index{Workshop prompt for learners}

Workshop prompt for learners

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why Series B changes the stack}

Speaker 1: The jump to Series B is about scale—200 people, multi-region support windows, and customers who read every appendix of the MSA. Speaker 2: Those customers expect 24/7 coverage, verifiable SOC 2 controls and contractual uptime remedies. Speaker 1: Investors simultaneously expect you to model spend 18 months out, so every SKU needs a forecast line and a justification. Speaker 2: That's why the stack becomes an enterprise nervous system instead of a patchwork of founder credit-card tools.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\end{itemize}

\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Board pressure to forecast spend with 18-month runway discipline.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\index{Reference architecture}

Speaker 2: A Snowflake lakehouse plus dbt models bring both worlds together for finance and customer health dashboards. Speaker 1: Add CLM for legal workflows and a SIEM/EDR pairing so every action shows up in the audit trail.
