\subsection*{Slide 8: Cost modelling worksheet structure}

\index{Cost modelling worksheet structure}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Let’s unpack the worksheet—it’s five tabs so finance, IT and GTM leaders stay in sync. Speaker 2: Inventory captures system owners, renewal dates and SKUs so nothing auto-renews in the shadows. Speaker 1: Seats \& tiers records current counts plus the trigger that forces an upgrade—headcount, compliance or product launch. Speaker 2: Projects tracks partner statements of work so you can capitalise or amortise where appropriate. Speaker 1: Scenario levers and risk offsets close the loop, showing how investments avoid penalties or headcount hires.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Cost modelling worksheet structure

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tab 1: Inventory – list systems, contract owners, renewal dates and SKUs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tab 2: Seats \& tiers – capture current counts, expansion triggers and unit costs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tab 3: Projects – implementation partners, statements of work, depreciation.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tab 4: Scenario levers – ARR growth, headcount plans, support coverage tiers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tab 5: Risk offsets – penalties avoided, staff savings, required compliance controls.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Example worksheet levers}

\index{Example worksheet levers}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: The levers tab is where your plan lives or dies. Speaker 2: Add a headcount scenario and watch Salesforce and ServiceNow costs adjust automatically. Speaker 1: When a new SOC 2 customer appears, the SIEM storage and support agents increase; the worksheet calculates the hit instantly. Speaker 2: If you spin up EMEA operations, Workato recipes and DocuSign compliance packs switch on—again, the formulas push the delta into the summary. Speaker 1: Don’t forget to document savings too; automation or product sunsets should feed the buffer instead of disappearing.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Example worksheet levers

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Headcount growth: +25 GTM seats = +\$1,800/month Salesforce uplift.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item New SOC 2 customer: adds \$600/month for additional SIEM storage + 2 ServiceNow agents.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item EMEA expansion: Workato adds 2 recipes; DocuSign adds eIDAS compliance pack.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Churn risk: removing 10\% low-touch customers cuts ServiceNow digital channels by \$400/month.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automation win: retiring legacy ETL saves \$700/month, funding CLM workflow bots.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Workshop prompt}

\index{Workshop prompt}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: For the workshop, we’ll map your current stack to this reference model. Speaker 2: Populate the worksheet with real owners, renewal dates and seat counts—no guesses. Speaker 1: Run best and worst ARR cases with a 15\% contingency so the board sees you’ve pressure-tested the plan. Speaker 2: Document the top integration risks and the mitigation you’ll fund with that buffer. Speaker 1: Close with a two-slide executive summary: one for spend, one for risk posture—it becomes your board pack insert.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Workshop prompt

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map your current stack against the reference architecture and highlight gaps.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Populate the worksheet tabs with your own seat counts and integration owners.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stress-test spend with best/worst-case ARR scenarios and 15\% contingency.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify the top three integration risks and document mitigation actions.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Present back a two-slide executive summary for board or investor readouts.
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation}
\index{Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation}
Slide 1 — Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation Shadow IT is not a villain; it's a neon sign flashing "your teams are hungry to solve problems." And banning every unsanctioned app just...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation}

\index{Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 1 — Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation Speaker 1: [energetic] Shadow IT is not a villain; it's a neon sign flashing "your teams are hungry to solve problems." Speaker 2: And banning every unsanctioned app just drives the experiments deeper underground, with zero telemetry. Speaker 1: Our job tonight is to channel that curiosity into a safe runway—guardrails, not handcuffs. Speaker 2: Because when you give people space to prototype responsibly, innovation and compliance can actually coexist.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Shadow IT and Low-Code Experimentation

Empower creativity without losing control

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why shadow IT happens}

\index{Why shadow IT happens}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 2 — Why shadow IT happens Speaker 1: Product managers see customer churn in real time and reach for whatever no-code tool plugs the hole fastest. Speaker 2: Meanwhile the official backlog is negotiating infrastructure upgrades, so "just wait" feels like career suicide. Speaker 1: Vendors don’t help—they wrap admin rights in cheerful free trials and suddenly payroll data lives in a hobby project. Speaker 2: It's human nature—if the official solution takes 6 months and the workaround takes 6 minutes, guess which one wins? Speaker 1: And lending out an "innocent" workaround is like handing over your car keys for a corner-store run that somehow ends in Vegas selfies.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why shadow IT happens

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Teams need quick wins while official backlogs stretch for quarters.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Low-code tools advertise drag-and-drop miracles and free tiers that bypass procurement.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Vendors bundle "starter" admin roles that feel harmless until production data lands inside.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item It's human nature—if the official solution takes 6 months and the workaround takes 6 minutes, guess which one wins?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Lend someone a "temporary" tool and it's like handing over your car keys for a corner-store run that somehow ends in Vegas photos.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Upside of sanctioned tinkering}

\index{Upside of sanctioned tinkering}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 3 — Upside of sanctioned tinkering Speaker 1: When we bless experimentation, prototypes become user research assets instead of rogue spreadsheets. Speaker 2: Remember that ops dashboard? They pulled support tickets, customer health scores, and renewal dates into one view that saved two hours of manual reporting every day. Speaker 1: Engineering would still be scoping the request; the team shipped it over a weekend and proved the value instantly. Speaker 2: Plus, citizen developers learn to speak API and process in the same sentence—it’s career development wrapped in delivery. Speaker 1: And when experiments are visible, finance finally gets data to justify the headcount or tooling upgrades the team has been whispering about.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Upside of sanctioned tinkering

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Rapid prototypes surface requirements before engineering commits sprints.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Business teams build dashboards, forms and automations that unblock frontline work.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Take that ops team dashboard—they pulled support ticket data, customer health scores, and renewal dates into one view that saved 2 hours of manual reporting daily.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Low-code playbooks cultivate citizen developers who speak both process and platform.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Early experiments become evidence for future budget and hiring conversations.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Risk: access sprawl and data leakage}

\index{Risk: access sprawl and data leakage}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 4 — Risk: access sprawl and data leakage Speaker 1: The dark side is permissions that balloon faster than anyone can track. Speaker 2: Suddenly marketing’s prototype syncs customer PII into someone’s personal Google Drive because the connector shipped with "full access". Speaker 1: And here’s the kicker—no one realizes until the first security audit and you’re explaining the phantom admin account. Speaker 1: Incident responders then chase ghosts—no runbooks, no system owner, just an error email at 2 a.m. Speaker 2: Meanwhile, the "free" tier quietly locks in your data—premium exports, surprise licensing, and compliance gaps galore. Speaker 1: And remember, many contracts and privacy laws explicitly forbid moving data to unsanctioned systems. Ignorance won’t save you during a GDPR or SOX review.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Risk: access sprawl and data leakage

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Over-permissioned connectors replicate sensitive data into personal accounts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Shadow integrations create blind spots for incident response and continuity plans.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Free tiers feel safe until lock-in hits: export limits, premium connectors, and licensing creep once the pilot succeeds.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Untracked API keys or webhook secrets violate customer contracts and regional laws—think GDPR data residency and SOX evidence trails.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Support teams inherit break/fix duties for stacks they have never seen before.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Cautionary tale: the Slack admin summer}

\index{Cautionary tale: the Slack admin summer}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 5 — Cautionary tale: the Slack admin summer Speaker 1: True story: an intern built a workflow bot to celebrate customer renewals. Speaker 2: Adorable—until they ticked "Workspace Admin" for every channel lead because "permissions are annoying". Speaker 1: Within days a curious contractor explored the new menu and archived the finance history channel. Speaker 2: Cue frantic tickets to Slack support, legal drafting disclosure emails, and the CTO spending Sunday rebuilding export logs. Enthusiasm needs seatbelts. Speaker 1: Also, three years of quarterly reports vanished—the CFO's expression was... memorable.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Cautionary tale: the Slack admin summer

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item An enthusiastic intern spun up a workflow app and, "to save time," granted Workspace Admin to every channel lead.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item A week later a contractor accidentally deleted finance archives while exploring new buttons.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Recovery required Slack support, legal notifications and a surprise weekend sprint to rebuild audit logs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item The lesson: enthusiasm without guardrails equals overtime and apology tours. Three years of quarterly reports, gone—the CFO's expression was... memorable.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Access guardrails that scale}

\index{Access guardrails that scale}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 6 — Access guardrails that scale Speaker 1: The fix is to engineer permission hygiene into the platform. Speaker 2: Start with role blueprints—builder, reviewer, auditor—and make them the only options in production tenants. Speaker 1: Provision through SSO groups so offboarding a leaver takes seconds and leaves an audit trail. Speaker 2: And yes, insist on data classification labels that literally stop exports of customer health scores or payroll files. Speaker 1: Any emergency elevation should ping the owner and expire automatically; we treat admin rights like temporary visas. Speaker 2: Because permanent admin is forever—and auditors have memories like elephants wearing spreadsheets.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Access guardrails that scale

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Default to least-privilege roles mapped to personas (builder, reviewer, auditor).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate provisioning via SSO groups so revoking access is one click, not 19 emails.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enforce data classification tags that block exports of regulated information.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Log every elevated permission grant and require manager sign-off within 24 hours.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat emergency admin like temporary visas—because permanent admin is forever and auditors never forget.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Safe sandboxes for experimentation}

\index{Safe sandboxes for experimentation}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 7 — Safe sandboxes for experimentation Speaker 1: Guardrails don’t mean boring. Give teams playgrounds with sanitized data and disposable connectors. Speaker 2: Picture this: finance gets a dedicated Tableau workspace, anonymized revenue data, connectors to approved databases, and templates that auto-expire after 90 days. Speaker 1: Golden templates save hours—they come preloaded with logging, naming conventions and "who to call" notes. Speaker 2: Also, route integrations through service accounts so when someone leaves, production tokens aren’t tied to their inbox. Speaker 1: Bonus points for running quarterly hack nights with platform engineers coaching—experimentation becomes a team sport, not a secret hobby.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Safe sandboxes for experimentation

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Offer dedicated dev tenants with scrubbed datasets and disposable connectors.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide golden templates that bake in audit logging, alerts and naming conventions.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Picture this: finance gets a dedicated Tableau workspace with anonymized revenue data, pre-configured connectors to approved databases, and templates that auto-expire after 90 days.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use API gateways or service accounts with scoped tokens instead of personal credentials.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule quarterly "citizen dev" hack nights with platform engineers coaching in real time.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Lightweight governance rituals}

\index{Lightweight governance rituals}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 8 — Lightweight governance rituals Speaker 1: Process-wise, start with a three-question intake form: what problem does this solve, what data does it touch, and who owns it when things break? Speaker 2: Then schedule a fortnightly thirty-minute huddle where platform, security and the builders review anything new. Speaker 1: Document outcomes in a living catalogue so support knows what exists and what tier of help it gets. Speaker 2: Feed notable risks into the enterprise register; executives hate surprises, but they love trendlines that show you’re steering the ship.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Lightweight governance rituals

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Publish a three-question intake form: what problem does this solve, what data does it touch, and who owns it when things break?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run fortnightly review huddles to bless launches, flag risks and share learnings.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain a living catalogue of approved tools with support tiers and renewal dates.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie shadow IT discoveries into risk registers so executives see trends, not surprises.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Observability and assurance}

\index{Observability and assurance}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 9 — Observability and assurance Speaker 1: If experimentation is invisible, risk teams default to "no". So wire these platforms into your logging stack. Speaker 2: Track the basics—"47 active low-code apps, 12 orphaned flows closed last quarter, 4-hour average response for connector issues"—so you can prove stewardship with data. Speaker 1: Run tabletop drills where a connector token is compromised. Watch who notices, who has the keys, and how fast you respond. Speaker 2: Then teach those lessons during onboarding so newcomers learn the approved way to tinker from day one.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Observability and assurance

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Instrument low-code platforms with central logging and anomaly alerts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track metrics like "47 active low-code apps, 12 orphaned flows closed last quarter, 4-hour average response for connector issues."
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Conduct tabletop exercises simulating connector breaches and revoked tokens.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Feed findings into onboarding so new hires learn "how we experiment here" on day one.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: How shadow IT surfaces}

\index{How shadow IT surfaces}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 10 — How shadow IT surfaces Speaker 1: Detection isn’t just gut instinct; network monitoring lights up when new SaaS domains start siphoning data. Speaker 2: Finance helps too—mystery \$49 charges and annual renewals on personal cards are the canary in the coal mine. Speaker 1: CASB dashboards and identity logs show which OAuth grants appeared without going through the service catalog. Speaker 2: When someone raises a hand about a rogue tool, celebrate the find first, then partner on the fix. Curiosity beats cover-ups.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

How shadow IT surfaces

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Network monitoring flags unfamiliar SaaS domains and unsanctioned API calls.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Finance spots recurring credit card charges and expense reports for "mystery" tools.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item CASB and identity logs reveal OAuth grants outside of approved registries.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Encourage teams to self-report discoveries—celebrate the find before fixing the gap.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Roles, traits and career pathways}

\index{Roles, traits and career pathways}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Slide 11 — Roles, traits and career pathways Speaker 1: The stewards here are often platform engineers or automation leads who love building tooling as much as guardrails. Speaker 2: They partner with business technologists—the ops analyst who can storyboard a process and translate it into a safe low-code pattern. Speaker 1: Governance analysts sharpen their empathy, learning to say "yes, if" and maturing into risk leaders who are still pro-experimentation. Speaker 2: And the curious citizen developers? With mentoring they grow into solution architects who mentor the next wave of tinkerers.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Roles, traits and career pathways

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Platform engineers and automation leads curate guardrails while enabling new builders.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Business technologists or ops analysts translate problems into safe low-code patterns.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Governance analysts grow into risk leads by shaping policy with pragmatic empathy.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Curious tinkerers progress from citizen devs to official solution architects who mentor others.
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Budgeting and FinOps for Start-ups}
\index{Budgeting and FinOps for Start-ups}
Welcome to our deep dive into budgeting and FinOps for Sarah's start-up. We're here to prove that disciplined cost management can coexist with ambitious product roadmaps. Think of this...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Budgeting and FinOps for Start-ups}

\index{Budgeting and FinOps for Start-ups}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [upbeat] Welcome to our deep dive into budgeting and FinOps for Sarah's start-up. Speaker 2: We're here to prove that disciplined cost management can coexist with ambitious product roadmaps. Speaker 1: Think of this session as a playbook for stretching every credit without throttling innovation. Speaker 2: And we promise to keep it tactical—no enterprise finance jargon required.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Budgeting and FinOps for Start-ups

Stretch every credit while protecting runway

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Session objectives}

\index{Session objectives}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [curious] First, let's anchor what participants should walk away with. Speaker 2: They need a FinOps mindset sized for fewer than 50 people, not a Fortune 500 bureaucracy. Speaker 1: We also connect tooling spend directly to runway conversations so finance, product and investors see the same numbers. Speaker 2: Finally, everyone practices spotting optimisation levers before renewals lock in waste.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Session objectives

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Understand FinOps principles tailored to sub-50 person teams.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Translate infrastructure and tooling spend into runway scenarios.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Build a cadence for monitoring usage and renegotiating credits.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: FinOps mindset in the first 24 months}

\index{FinOps mindset in the first 24 months}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [thoughtful] A FinOps mindset in year one starts with acknowledging cash is your scarcest resource. Speaker 2: Exactly—so we lay out guardrails before Sarah automates every workflow or signs annual commits. Speaker 1: Documenting cost taxonomy sounds dull, but it stops engineering and finance from arguing over which budget a new tool hits. Speaker 2: And those living forecasts? They're the proof investors need that the team is steering spend deliberately.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

FinOps mindset in the first 24 months

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cash is the constraint; design guardrails before scaling automation.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align product, finance and engineering on a single cost taxonomy.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document who owns pricing decisions for each platform or vendor.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat forecasts as living artefacts reviewed with investor updates.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Mapping cash runway with tooling}

\index{Mapping cash runway with tooling}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [analytical] When we map runway, we anchor on a simple burn formula that everybody can recite. Speaker 2: Then we separate must-have spend from experiment budgets so product bets don't quietly cannibalise payroll. Speaker 1: Calling out contractual cliffs keeps Sarah from being surprised by auto-renewals or seat minimums. Speaker 2: And the shared dashboard keeps stakeholders aligned on which customers and features actually drive the bill.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Mapping cash runway with tooling

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with burn formula: (opening cash - committed spend) ÷ monthly burn.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Categorise costs into keep-the-lights-on vs. experiment budgets.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight contractual cliffs: annual renewals, seat minimums, auto-renewals.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use a shared dashboard to show spend per customer or feature flag.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Making cloud credits last}

\index{Making cloud credits last}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [energised] Credits can feel like free money, but the expiry dates creep up fast. Speaker 2: That's why we inventory every cloud provider perk and match workloads carefully. Speaker 1: Low-risk environments soak up those credits first while we tune rightsizing and scheduling tactics. Speaker 2: Budget alerts at 60, 80 and 100 percent stop panic fire drills by catching drift early.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Making cloud credits last

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Catalogue every provider credit, expiry date and eligible workloads.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Deploy credits to low-risk environments first (sandbox, QA).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set budget alerts at 60/80/100\% of expected usage to course-correct.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pair credits with optimisation tactics: rightsizing, scheduling shutdowns, spot usage.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Tooling usage monitoring toolkit}

\index{Tooling usage monitoring toolkit}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [practical] Monitoring usage is the boring hero work that keeps costs tame. Speaker 2: Centralising billing exports means Sarah stops copy-pasting invoices at midnight. Speaker 1: Tags and labels turn raw bills into insight—suddenly you know the growth team triggered last month's spike. Speaker 2: Weekly digests and variance triggers create a rhythm so nobody is surprised by the finance meeting.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Tooling usage monitoring toolkit

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Centralise billing exports in a warehouse or spreadsheet.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Instrument services with tags/labels for team, feature and environment.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate weekly cost digests to Slack/email for accountable owners.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Trigger lightweight reviews when variances exceed 15\% week-on-week.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Monthly stack spend drill}

\index{Monthly stack spend drill}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [engaging] Here's the concrete spend drill we run in workshops. Speaker 2: Learners see how credits offset AWS bills, while cash still flows to collaboration, monitoring and support tools. Speaker 1: The contingency line normalises setting aside 10 percent for surprises instead of hoping they never happen. Speaker 2: We deliberately show the total cash outlay so teams link the numbers back to runway, not just accrual accounting.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Monthly stack spend drill

Tool / ServiceSeat or usage assumptionMonthly cost (AUD)

Google Workspace Business Standard12 seats @ \$11\$132

AWS compute \& storageForecast 450 credits, 70\% burn\$0 (credit drawdown)

Datadog monitoring3 engineer seats @ \$27\$81

Customer support platform6 agents @ \$20\$120

Contingency / experiment buffer10\% of baseline\$33

Total cash outlay\$366 (credits cover \$150 value)

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Workshop instructions}

\index{Workshop instructions}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [directive] During the exercise, we ask each team to clone the drill with their own stack assumptions. Speaker 2: As they tweak seat counts and usage, the guardrails force trade-offs—keep the SOC tool or hire a contractor? Speaker 1: The optimisation lever per tool becomes an action list for the next quarter. Speaker 2: It also builds muscle memory for renegotiating or automating before the finance team has to step in.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Workshop instructions

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Duplicate the drill with your own stack and contract terms.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Adjust assumptions until your cash outlay stays within guardrails.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify one optimisation lever per tool (renegotiate, downgrade, automate).
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Guardrails \& red flags}

\index{Guardrails \& red flags}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [cautionary] We also spotlight the red flags Sarah will meet in real life. Speaker 2: Multi-year deals feel flattering, but at pre-Series A they usually mortgage optionality. Speaker 1: Auto-renewals are sneaky, so we model calendar holds as part of the FinOps ritual. Speaker 2: And when founders ignore chargeback data, they lose credibility fast with both finance leads and investors.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Guardrails \& red flags

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Vendor pushes multi-year deal before product-market fit—counter with quarterly.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Usage spikes without revenue signal? Pause automation rollouts immediately.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Silent auto-renewals: set calendar holds 60 days prior to renewal dates.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Founders ignoring chargeback data erodes trust with finance and investors.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Linking to investor conversations}

\index{Linking to investor conversations}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [confident] All this work feeds directly into investor conversations. Speaker 2: Monthly scorecards show spend versus forecast and how many credits remain, signalling control. Speaker 1: When Sarah can say "rightsizing bought us two extra months of runway," the room pays attention. Speaker 2: Inviting observers to FinOps reviews turns a potential grilling into a collaboration ahead of the next raise.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Linking to investor conversations

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share FinOps scorecard in monthly updates: spend vs. forecast, credits remaining.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Demonstrate how optimisations extended runway (e.g., +2 months from rightsizing).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use variance explanations to reinforce control over GTM and product strategy.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Invite observers to quarterly FinOps reviews to build confidence pre-Series A.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\index{Action plan for Sarah}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: [motivating] We close with an action plan Sarah can run immediately. Speaker 2: Weekly cost reviews with engineering and finance build the drumbeat. Speaker 1: The central ledger for credits, renewals and owners stops information from living in someone's inbox. Speaker 2: Revisiting guardrails at each funding milestone keeps FinOps aligned with the pace of growth instead of blocking it.

Action plan for Sarah

\section{Vendor Management Rhythms}
\index{Vendor Management Rhythms}
Vendor Management Rhythms — Narrative High-growth teams lean on vendors to fill capability gaps long before they can hire specialists. That leverage only works when everyone is working to...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Vendor Management Rhythms}

\index{Vendor Management Rhythms}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Vendor Management Rhythms — Narrative High-growth teams lean on vendors to fill capability gaps long before they can hire specialists. That leverage only works when everyone is working to the same beat. Rituals create shared expectations about responsiveness, decision velocity, and quality gates. Without them, a managed service provider can unknowingly slow the roadmap or miss critical context about upcoming launches. This segment frames cadence as a strategic control system rather than polite catch-ups. We also remind founders that rhythms reduce emotional escalations. When partners know there is a weekly forum to raise blockers, they do not resort to panicked emails at midnight. When the leadership team sees trend data every month, they can intervene early instead of issuing broad-brush ultimatums. Process gives both sides psychological safety to be candid about risks.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Vendor Management Rhythms

Keep outsourced partners aligned with start-up velocity

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ever had a vendor who thinks "urgent" means "next week"? Rhythms fix that.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why cadence matters}

\index{Why cadence matters}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Establishing cadence rituals Coming out of the "why rhythms matter" segment, we hand learners a concrete operating drumbeat they can deploy on Monday. We outline a three-tier rhythm that keeps partners plugged into strategy and execution. Weekly operations syncs are deliberately short and tactical: review ticket queues, note any SLA breaches, and unblock near-term tasks. Monthly service reviews go a level higher to interrogate trend lines, incident learnings, and improvement experiments. Quarterly business reviews reconnect the relationship to company strategy, budgets, and roadmap shifts. Emphasise that cadence is anchored to meaningful triggers. Align the weekly call before your release deploys, schedule the monthly review after financial close so real cost data is available, and run the quarterly session ahead of contract renewal windows. When rituals connect to existing beats, the right stakeholders attend prepared instead of treating meetings as optional. Close every meeting by logging owners, deadlines, and notes in the shared workspace so momentum compounds instead of evaporating between calls.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why cadence matters

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Vendors extend your team but follow different incentives and clocks.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Predictable rituals surface risks early before they hit customers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Rhythm builds trust: expectations, response times, and accountability tighten.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Core meeting cadence}

\index{Core meeting cadence}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Running the weekly ops sync The weekly sync should feel like a high-signal stand-up, not a status monologue. Encourage learners to cap the session at 30 minutes with a three-slide deck: performance snapshot, escalations, and upcoming changes. Assign owners to every yellow or red metric before the call ends and log due dates in the shared tracker. Anything without a name or deadline will resurface as an incident later. Use the final minutes for a "no surprises" scan. Ask explicitly about launches, audits, marketing campaigns, or staffing changes that could impact capacity. Offer a concrete prompt: "We're launching the Black Friday campaign next week and expect 10x traffic—can your monitoring handle the alert volume?" That habit gives vendors permission to flag constraints before they become outages and reinforces that the start-up wants partnership, not heroics. It also prevents the 3 a.m. vendor panic call that starts with "We didn't know you were deploying today...".

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Core meeting cadence

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Weekly ops sync (30 min): Ticket queues, blockers, near-term deliverables.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Monthly service review (60 min): KPI scorecard, incident retros, improvement backlog.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Quarterly business review (90 min): Strategic alignment, contract health, roadmap shifts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Anchor rituals to billing or release cycles so stakeholders show up prepared.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document outcomes in the shared workspace—no meeting closes without follow-up artefacts.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Vendor security assessments}

\index{Vendor security assessments}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Building a meaningful scorecard Scorecards convert gut feel into shared evidence. Coach learners to combine SLA/SLR metrics—response time, resolution time, uptime—with adoption and satisfaction data such as NPS from internal stakeholders or product usage analytics. Leading indicators like backlog age, staffing ratios, and change failure rate provide early warning signals before contractual breaches occur. Stress the importance of data hygiene. Partners should pull metrics from a single source of truth, snapshot them before the review, and annotate anomalies. Colour coding helps executives parse quickly, but it must link to predefined thresholds that automatically trigger escalation or executive awareness. The scorecard becomes a living document for accountability. Show an example template to make the abstract concrete: availability $\ge$99.9\% stays green, 99.5–99.89\% is yellow, anything lower turns red. Pair that with first-response targets (green <15 minutes for P1 tickets), change failure rate bands (<10\% green, 10–20\% yellow), backlog age for critical tickets (<3 days green), stakeholder NPS ($\ge$50 green), and compliance evidence status. When facilitators can point to six crisp metrics with thresholds, learners understand how to translate principles into dashboards.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Vendor security assessments

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat security reviews as recurring rituals, not a one-off procurement hurdle.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Require up-to-date SOC 2 / ISO 27001 evidence and map controls to your data flows.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run data-handling tabletop drills: breach notification timing, encryption practices, off-boarding.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align vendor access reviews with your internal identity governance cadence.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Contract negotiation basics}

\index{Contract negotiation basics}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Monthly service review ritual Monthly reviews zoom out far enough to connect operational health with strategic priorities. Encourage facilitators to lead with trend lines: three months of SLA performance, cost variance, and satisfaction scores. From there, walk through incidents—what we learned, how remediations are tracking, and whether they require executive support. Capacity planning is a key section. Challenge vendors to bring forward-looking staffing plans, upcoming maintenance windows, and assumptions about ticket volumes. Close the review by committing to two or three improvement experiments, assigning owners, and noting when results will be assessed. That loop converts the meeting from reporting theatre into a driver of continuous improvement. Do not forget to celebrate the wins. A quick shout-out for the vendor engineer who crushed the migration or the analyst who spotted a fraud pattern keeps the relationship human and motivates continued excellence.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Contract negotiation basics

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Define the difference: SLAs commit to performance, SLRs describe reporting—negotiate both.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie penalty clauses to business impact (credit for downtime, remediation timelines, escalation paths).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Specify exit strategies: data export formats, transition assistance, knowledge transfer windows.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture renewal notice periods and price-uplift caps inside the master services agreement.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Cultural fit checkpoints}

\index{Cultural fit checkpoints}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Make-vs-buy as a living decision Start-ups often make an initial outsourcing decision under extreme time pressure. Remind learners that the calculus shifts as product-market fit solidifies, customer expectations rise, and internal capabilities mature. A quarterly make-vs-buy checkpoint keeps leaders honest about whether the vendor still unlocks speed or has become drag. Walk through the dimensions: total cost of ownership (subscription, integration, shadow teams, compliance overhead), strategic control (IP sensitivity, customer intimacy, regulatory obligations), and risk posture (single points of failure, vendor financial health, data residency). Encourage teams to set quantitative thresholds—like customer volume or margin targets—that trigger formal RFPs or insourcing investigations. Pair the framework with contrasting case studies so learners can see the decision in different domains. Alongside payments, explore customer support: adopting Zendesk gets you macros, analytics, and AI triage overnight, while building an internal support organisation requires hiring, coaching, and tooling for QA. The make-vs-buy answer can shift over time; maybe you start with Zendesk to launch fast, then insource tier-two support once ticket complexity justifies bespoke workflows.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Cultural fit checkpoints

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Observe vendor-team rituals: stand-ups, retros, documentation habits—do they match your pace?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Meet the delivery leads who will work day-to-day, not just the sales crew.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align on communication norms (channels, response times, decision logs) before signing.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use pilot projects or trial sprints to test collaboration chemistry safely.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Designing the scorecard}

\index{Designing the scorecard}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Stripe versus build-your-own payments Use the payments example to make the framework tangible. Stripe lets a lean team accept money quickly with globally resilient infrastructure, built-in fraud tooling, and compliance coverage. The trade-off is ongoing transaction fees and limited influence over the roadmap. Building in-house grants total control but requires hiring specialised engineers, gaining PCI certification, and running 24/7 monitoring from day one. Invite learners to map the four lenses: speed to market, cost structure, control, and risk. Highlight that "cost" is more than fees—it is also opportunity cost of delayed launches. Similarly, "control" includes the ability to adapt to local regulations or bespoke checkout flows. Close by asking the cohort when they would trigger a revisit—perhaps crossing \$100M GMV or expanding into complex billing models. Prompt discussion with a forward-looking question: if Stripe announced a material price hike tomorrow, which leading indicators would tell you it's time to diversify payment providers before customers feel pain?

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Designing the scorecard

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Blend SLAs/SLRs (uptime, response) with adoption and satisfaction signals.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track leading indicators: backlog age, staffing ratios, change failure rate.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pull data from shared dashboards; freeze snapshots before each review.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use traffic-light status to trigger escalations and executive visibility.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example metric bands: API response time <200ms (green), 200–500ms (yellow), >500ms (red).
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Scorecard template snapshot}

\index{Scorecard template snapshot}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Documentation and accountability habits Documentation is the connective tissue that keeps vendor rhythms effective as teams grow. Reinforce that every meeting produces a shared note: agenda, decisions, owners, and deadlines. Store artefacts in a central workspace alongside scorecards, contracts, runbooks, and escalation matrices. That repository becomes the onboarding kit for new hires and a safeguard during audits or fundraising. Encourage lightweight automation. Integrate the action log with your ticketing tool or CRM so reminders fire without manual chasing. Use templated scorecards and meeting notes to reduce admin overhead. The goal is to make good governance the default path, not additional labour. Remind facilitators that future-you will thank present-you for taking notes—future-you is notoriously impatient with mystery decisions.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Scorecard template snapshot

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Availability (SLA): Green $\ge$ 99.9\%, Yellow 99.5–99.89\%, Red < 99.5\%.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item First-response time (SLR): Green <15m P1 / <1h P2, Yellow double the target, Red >4x target.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Change failure rate: Green <10\%, Yellow 10–20\%, Red >20\%.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Backlog age (critical tickets): Green <3 days, Yellow 3–5, Red >5.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Stakeholder NPS: Green $\ge$50, Yellow 20–49, Red <20.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compliance status: Green = audits current, Yellow = evidence pending, Red = gap identified.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Running the weekly sync}

\index{Running the weekly sync}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Key takeaways for the cohort Wrap the segment by connecting rituals to resilience. When vendors share the same cadence as the core team, surprises shrink and response times improve. Scorecards turn debates into collaborative problem solving. A living make-vs-buy framework ensures the relationship continues to serve strategy rather than legacy decisions. Leave learners with an action plan: schedule the next three vendor touchpoints, refresh the scorecard template, and document the thresholds that would trigger an insourcing review. These steps show that governance can be lightweight yet powerful when it is intentional. Close with a gut-check question: could a new team member understand your vendor relationships from the documentation alone? If not, the rhythms outlined here will highlight where to invest next.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Running the weekly sync

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with a three-slide deck: numbers, escalations, upcoming changes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Confirm ownership on every red/yellow metric with due dates.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture blockers requiring internal help (access, decisions, budget).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Close with "no surprises" check: launches, audits, peak demand.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Monthly service review ritual}

\index{Monthly service review ritual}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Building a vendor security muscle Security diligence cannot be outsourced entirely to procurement. Coach learners to treat vendor assessments as ongoing hygiene: request SOC 2 or ISO 27001 reports annually, review penetration-test summaries, and map each control to the data the vendor actually touches. Pair compliance evidence with practical walkthroughs of how encryption keys are managed, how access is revoked when staff leave, and how incident notifications will flow to your team. Encourage startups to run joint tabletop exercises. Simulate a compromised credential or data-exfiltration alert to confirm who leads, who communicates, and how regulators or customers are notified. These rehearsals expose gaps in logging, monitoring, or contractual commitments. Fold the outputs into the same scorecards that track uptime so security posture has equal visibility.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Monthly service review ritual

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Walk through the scorecard trend lines, not just last month's value.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight incident learnings and verify action item closure.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Revisit capacity forecasts and staffing assumptions for the next 60 days.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Agree on 2-3 experiments or optimisations before the next review.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Celebrate wins and recognise vendor team contributions to maintain relationship health.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Crisis management drills}

\index{Crisis management drills}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Negotiating contracts that protect momentum Contracts are operating tools, not just legal formalities. Help learners differentiate service-level agreements (SLAs) that commit to performance and service-level reports (SLRs) that provide transparency. Push for clear remediation timelines, service credits tied to business impact, and escalation ladders that reach executives when targets are missed. Include exit strategies in the negotiation checklist. Define how data is exported, how knowledge transfer occurs, and how long the vendor will support your migration. Cap annual price uplifts and require advance notice for material changes. When founders see contracts as living documents aligned to their rhythms, they negotiate clauses that keep partners responsive instead of adversarial.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Crisis management drills

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pre-build a shared incident channel, escalation ladder, and on-call rotation map.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run joint simulations for vendor outage, data breach, and sudden demand spikes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Define decision authority for rollback, customer comms, and regulatory notifications.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture learnings in a post-mortem template shared across companies.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 12: Make-vs-buy guardrails}

\index{Make-vs-buy guardrails}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Evaluating cultural fit before you sign Cultural fit sounds squishy, but it dictates execution speed. Encourage founders to meet the delivery leads, project managers, and senior ICs who will work with them daily. Observe how the vendor runs stand-ups, documents decisions, and handles retrospectives. If their operating cadence feels slower or more hierarchical than yours, that friction will surface the first time you need a hotfix on a weekend. Suggest trial sprints or limited-scope pilots to test collaboration chemistry safely. Track responsiveness to Slack pings, clarity of written updates, and willingness to share bad news quickly. Those signals matter as much as price because they predict whether the vendor can blend into your existing rituals without constant policing.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Make-vs-buy guardrails

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Reassess quarterly: does outsourcing still unlock speed or introduce drag?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Model total cost: subscription, integration effort, shadow teams, compliance overhead.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Evaluate strategic control: IP sensitivity, customer intimacy, regulatory obligations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document thresholds that trigger RFPs or insourcing explorations.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 13: Case study: Stripe vs. building payments}

\index{Case study: Stripe vs. building payments}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Preparing for vendor crises Even the strongest vendor relationship will experience a wobble. Coach learners to pre-build an incident response playbook shared across organisations: who is on the escalation ladder, which Slack or Teams channel lights up first, and how decisions are documented in the heat of the moment. Align on authority to pause deployments, issue customer communications, and pull in legal or compliance. Encourage quarterly simulations that mirror their worst nightmares: outage during peak season, security breach, or sudden vendor staff attrition. After each drill, capture lessons learned and plug them into scorecards and retrospectives. The goal is to reduce reaction time when a real crisis hits and keep leadership confidence high even under pressure.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Case study: Stripe vs. building payments

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speed: Stripe SDKs let you launch in weeks; in-house build requires new headcount.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cost: Fees look high, but compare to hiring, PCI scope, 24/7 monitoring.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Control: Outsourcing reduces roadmap control; negotiate premium support for reliability.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Risk: Stripe's redundancy is proven; your custom stack must reach the same bar.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 14: Case study: Zendesk vs. building support ops}

\index{Case study: Zendesk vs. building support ops}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Case study: Zendesk vs. building support ops

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speed: Zendesk templates, macros, and AI triage accelerate support launch within days.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cost: Subscription plus integration vs. hiring, training, and managing a 24/7 support desk.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Control: Platform roadmap dictates feature availability; in-house team can craft bespoke workflows.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Risk: Vendor outage impacts customer service; internal team faces burnout without mature processes.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 15: Documentation + tooling}

\index{Documentation + tooling}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Documentation + tooling

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Centralise agendas, scorecards, and action logs in a shared workspace.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Version notes and decisions to protect institutional memory through turnover.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate reminders via your ticketing or CRM to chase overdue items.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Store vendor runbooks alongside incident response and onboarding guides.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Future you will thank present you for taking notes—future you is less patient with mystery decisions.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 16: Takeaways for founders}

\index{Takeaways for founders}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Takeaways for founders

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ritualise touchpoints so vendors feel part of the operating rhythm.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep scorecards visible—green metrics get celebrated, red ones trigger swift support.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Treat make-vs-buy as a living decision, not a one-off slide.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document relentlessly; future you (or your successor) will need the receipts.
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}
